texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To Vaughan Jones, for all he taught us about knot polynomials and life
texts.text: To Vaughan Jones, for all he taught us about knot polynomials and life

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. We find a single two-parameter skein relation on trivalent graphs, the quantum exceptional relation , that specializes to a skein relation associated to each exceptional Lie algebra (in the adjoint representation). If a slight strengthening of Deligne's conjecture on the existence of a (classical) exceptional series is true, then this relation holds for a new two-variable quantum exceptional polynomial, at least as a power series near 𝑞 = 1. The single quantum exceptional relation can be viewed as a deformation of the Jacobi relation, and implies a deformation of the Vogel relation that motivated the conjecture on the classical exceptional series.
texts.text: Abstract. We find a single two-parameter skein relation on trivalent graphs, the quantum exceptional relation , that specializes to a skein relation associated to each exceptional Lie algebra (in the adjoint representation). If a slight strengthening of Deligne's conjecture on the existence of a (classical) exceptional series is true, then this relation holds for a new two-variable quantum exceptional polynomial, at least as a power series near 𝑞 = 1. The single quantum exceptional relation can be viewed as a deformation of the Jacobi relation, and implies a deformation of the Vogel relation that motivated the conjecture on the classical exceptional series.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We find a conjectural basis for the space of diagrams with 𝑛 loose ends modulo the quantum exceptional relation for 𝑛 ≤ 6, with dimensions agreeing with the classical computations, and compute the matrix of inner products, and the quantum dimensions of idempotents. We use the skein relation to compute the conjectural quantum exceptional polynomial for many knots. In particular we determine (unconditionally) the values of the quantum polynomials for the exceptional Lie algebras on these knots. We can perform these computations for all links of Conway width less than 6, which includes all prime knots with 12 or fewer crossings. Finally, we prove several specialization results relating our conjectural family to certain quantum group categories, and conjecture a number of exceptional analogues of level-rank duality.
texts.text: We find a conjectural basis for the space of diagrams with 𝑛 loose ends modulo the quantum exceptional relation for 𝑛 ≤ 6, with dimensions agreeing with the classical computations, and compute the matrix of inner products, and the quantum dimensions of idempotents. We use the skein relation to compute the conjectural quantum exceptional polynomial for many knots. In particular we determine (unconditionally) the values of the quantum polynomials for the exceptional Lie algebras on these knots. We can perform these computations for all links of Conway width less than 6, which includes all prime knots with 12 or fewer crossings. Finally, we prove several specialization results relating our conjectural family to certain quantum group categories, and conjecture a number of exceptional analogues of level-rank duality.

texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are two main 2-variable knot polynomials: the HOMFLY-PT polynomial [FYH + 85, PT88] and the Kauffman polynomial [Kau90]. The HOMFLY-PT polynomial interpolates between the Reshetikhin-Turaev invariants [RT90] of the standard representations of the quantum groups 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔤𝔩 𝑛 ) , while the Kauffman polynomial does the same for the standard representations of 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔬 𝑛 ) (or, in a slightly different form, the standard representations of 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔰𝔭 𝑛 ) ). The two variable nature of these knot polynomials come from the two parameters 𝑛 and 𝑞 , but we need to replace the discrete parameter 𝑛 with a continuous one. One can think of this process in two steps: first construct a family of Lie algebra objects in symmetric ribbon categories which interpolate between the 𝔤𝔩 𝑛 yielding something called 𝔤𝔩 𝑡 [DMOS82], and second quantize these Lie algebra objects. This quantization can be done systematically using the Kontsevich integral [BN95], provided it converges.

The two variable nature of these knot polynomials come from the two parameters 𝑛 and 𝑞 , but we need to replace the discrete parameter 𝑛 with a continuous one. One can think of this process in two steps: first construct a family of Lie algebra objects in symmetric ribbon categories which interpolate between the 𝔤𝔩 𝑛 yielding something called 𝔤𝔩 𝑡 [DMOS82], and second quantize these Lie algebra objects. This quantization can be done systematically using the Kontsevich integral [BN95], provided it converges.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Work of Cvitanović, Vogel, Deligne, Cohen-de-Man, and others [Cvi08, Del96, DdM96, DG02, CdM96, Vog11, Vog99], can be summarized as a conjecture that there is a third 1-parameter family of Lie algebra objects in symmetric ribbon categories, called the (classical) exceptional series. This should interpolate between the adjoint representations of 𝔞 1, 𝔞 2, 𝔡 4, 𝔤 2, 𝔣 4, 𝔢 6, 𝔢 7, and 𝔢 8 in an appropriate sense. If this classical exceptional family exists, we should again expect a quantization yielding a 2-variable exceptional knot invariant 𝜉 . In this paper we give a conjectural skein

texts.orig: We can use these skein relations to do many computations. In particular, we are able to calculate the value of this knot polynomial (if it exists!) on all knots of Conway width 6, which includes all knots of 12 or fewer crossings. Unconditionally these give the first calculations of the Reshetikhin-Turaev knot invariants for the exceptional Lie algebras for most of these knots. (For 2- and 3-strand torus links and for algebraic/arborescent links such calculations can already be found by specializing the calculations done by Mironov-Mkrtchyan-Morozov [MMM16, MM16].) For example, we compute the following value for the left-handed trefoil for the exceptional series, and the Reshetikhin-Turaev invariant for 𝔢 8 is given by setting 𝑣 = 𝑞 5 and 𝑤 = 𝑞 -1 :

texts.orig: We begin with the symmetric ribbon category of Jacobi diagrams, Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 .
texts.text: We begin with the symmetric ribbon category of Jacobi diagrams, Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 1.1. Let 𝑅 be an algebra over Q , let 𝐷 be an element of 𝑅 and let 𝐵 be an invertible element of 𝑅 . (We use capital letters for these classical quantities; the corresponding quantum quantities will get lowercase letters.) The objects of Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 are collections of 𝑛 points on the line and the morphism space from 𝑛 to 𝑚 consists of 𝑅 -linear combinations of planar graphs in a rectangle with 𝑛 boundary points on the bottom, 𝑚 boundary points at the top, and consisting of trivalent vertices and 4-valent symmetric crossings 1 modulo planar isotopy, the usual Reidemeister relations for the symmetric crossing and trivalent vertex, and the following circle, lollipop, bigon, triangle, anti-symmetry of the vertex, symmetry of the Killing form, and Jacobi relations.

texts.orig: There's an equivalence of ribbon categories Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝑥𝐵 for any invertible 𝑥 ∈ 𝑅 given by isotoping each diagram so every trivalent vertex has two edges coming in from below and one edge going out above and then rescaling by 𝑥 for each Morse minimum and 𝑥 -1 for each Morse maximum. In particular, if 𝑅 = C then Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 does not depend on the choice of 𝐵 . A convenient normalization is 𝐵 = 12.
texts.text: There's an equivalence of ribbon categories Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝑥𝐵 for any invertible 𝑥 ∈ 𝑅 given by isotoping each diagram so every trivalent vertex has two edges coming in from below and one edge going out above and then rescaling by 𝑥 for each Morse minimum and 𝑥 -1 for each Morse maximum. In particular, if 𝑅 = C then Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 does not depend on the choice of 𝐵 . A convenient normalization is 𝐵 = 12.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If 𝔤 is a simple Lie algebra object in a symmetric ribbon category with dimension 𝐷 , by the diagram calculus the symmetric ribbon category of finite dimensional representations of 𝔤 is the target of a functor from Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 , where we interpret each strand as a copy of 𝔤 , each cup or cap using the Killing form or its inverse, each trivalent vertex as the Lie bracket, and each crossing as the usual interchange of tensor factors. The category Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 is somewhat poorly behaved since the space of 0-boundary point diagrams is infinite dimensional.

The category Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 is somewhat poorly behaved since the space of 0-boundary point diagrams is infinite dimensional.

texts.orig: Let us fix some notation. If C is a diagram category we denote the Hom space from 𝑘 points to ℓ points by C( 𝑘, 𝑙 ) and the invariant space C( 𝑘, 0 ) by C( 𝑘 ) . Since all our categories are ribbon, we can use duality to canonically identify C( 𝑘, 𝑙 ) = C( 𝑛 ) = C( 𝑘 ′ , 𝑙 ′ ) for any 𝑘 + 𝑙 = 𝑛 = 𝑘 ′ + 𝑙 ′ by using caps on the right to turn the outputs into inputs. We call this space the 𝑛 -box space . Of course to compose 𝑛 -boxes we need to be careful to turn the appropriate strands back upwards using cups. In some cases, if the notation for C already involves a parenthesis, we will instead use C 𝑛 to denote the 𝑛 -box space, but hopefully this should be clear from context.
texts.text: Let us fix some notation. If C is a diagram category we denote the Hom space from 𝑘 points to ℓ points by C( 𝑘, 𝑙 ) and the invariant space C( 𝑘, 0 ) by C( 𝑘 ) . Since all our categories are ribbon, we can use duality to canonically identify C( 𝑘, 𝑙 ) = C( 𝑛 ) = C( 𝑘 ′ , 𝑙 ′ ) for any 𝑘 + 𝑙 = 𝑛 = 𝑘 ′ + 𝑙 ′ by using caps on the right to turn the outputs into inputs. We call this space the 𝑛 -box space . Of course to compose 𝑛 -boxes we need to be careful to turn the appropriate strands back upwards using cups. In some cases, if the notation for C already involves a parenthesis, we will instead use C 𝑛 to denote the 𝑛 -box space, but hopefully this should be clear from context.

texts.orig: Conjecture 1.4 (Classical consistency) . So long as a certain finite set of non-zero polynomials in 𝜆 are invertible in 𝑅 , then 𝑅 injects into Exc 𝑅,𝜆 ( 0 ) as multiples of the empty diagram. In particular, any two ways of simplifying the same closed diagram to give an element of 𝑅 will give the same value.

If 𝐺 is one of the (super)groups on the right of Table 1, and 𝜆 is the value shown on the left (also equal to -6 / ℎ ∨ , where ℎ ∨ is the dual Coxeter number), then there is a full and dominant functor from Exc C ,𝜆 to the category of representations of the corresponding group on the right of Table 1.
texts.text: Proposition 1.6 (Classical specialization) . If 𝐺 is one of the (super)groups on the right of Table 1, and 𝜆 is the value shown on the left (also equal to -6 / ℎ ∨ , where ℎ ∨ is the dual Coxeter number), then there is a full and dominant functor from Exc C ,𝜆 to the category of representations of the corresponding group on the right of Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In particular, the category of representations of the corresponding group on the right of Table 1 is the Cauchy completion (i.e., additive and idempotent completion) of a quotient of Exc C ,𝜆 . This result is in the style of the first fundamental theorem of invariant theory. We would also like an explicit characterization of the kernel of this quotient in the style of the second fundamental theorem of invariant theory, which we do not have. If Conjecture 1.3 holds, then the kernel of this quotient can be characterized abstractly as the tensor ideal of negligible morphisms.
texts.text: In particular, the category of representations of the corresponding group on the right of Table 1 is the Cauchy completion (i.e., additive and idempotent completion) of a quotient of Exc C ,𝜆 . This result is in the style of the first fundamental theorem of invariant theory. We would also like an explicit characterization of the kernel of this quotient in the style of the second fundamental theorem of invariant theory, which we do not have. If Conjecture 1.3 holds, then the kernel of this quotient can be characterized abstractly as the tensor ideal of negligible morphisms.

texts.orig: Conjecture 1.7 (Classical semisimplicity) . There is a dense subset 𝑈 ⊂ C so that, for 𝜆 ∈ 𝑈 , the Cauchy completion of Exc C ,𝜆 is semisimple.
texts.text: Conjecture 1.7 (Classical semisimplicity) . There is a dense subset 𝑈 ⊂ C so that, for 𝜆 ∈ 𝑈 , the Cauchy completion of Exc C ,𝜆 is semisimple.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Here as elsewhere, we use the standard, not Zariski, topology on C .) We expect that in order for the Cauchy completion to be semisimple one needs to invert infinitely many polynomials. For example, for the Cauchy completion of GL 𝑡 to be semisimple you need to invert 𝑡 -𝑛 for every positive integer 𝑛 . From the dimension formulas of Cohen-de-Man and others, we might speculate that for classical semisimplicity it is enough to invert 𝑘𝜆 -ℓ for 𝑘, ℓ ∈ Z . It would follow from Conjecture 1.7 that the Cauchy completion of the generic category Exc C ( 𝜆 ) ,𝜆 is semisimple.

texts.orig: 1.2. Laurent polynomial conventions. The bulk of this paper deals with quantum versions of the sufficiency and consistency conjectures, now involving two variables 𝑣 and 𝑤 . 2 Before stating these conjectures we fix some notation for certain Laurent polynomials appearing in our formulas. On the one hand, we would like to use formulas that are irreducible as Laurent polynomials in 𝑣 and 𝑤 , so that we can easily read off which primes need to be inverted. On the other hand the Laurent polynomials that occur in our formulas are often even or odd under 𝑣 ↔ 𝑣 -1 and/or 𝑤 ↔ 𝑤 -1 , so we would also like our formulas to have this symmetry. So we often combine two irreducible factors into a single expression. For example, 𝑣 -𝑣 -1 = 𝑣 -1 ( 𝑣 -1 )( 𝑣 + 1 ) = ( 𝑣 1 2 -𝑣 -1 2 )( 𝑣 1 2 + 𝑣 -1 2 ) but there is no way to factor it into odd or even Laurent polynomials so we

texts.orig: 1.3. Quantum exceptional conjectures. We now turn to our main conjectures. Our first goal is to give a definition of a ribbon category QExc which looks like a quantum deformation of Exc . In particular we need skein relations which deform the Jacobi relation, the classical exceptional relation, and the symmetric relation between the overcrossing and undercrossing. Our specific choices of relations will be motivated below by our main theorems.
texts.text: 1.3. Quantum exceptional conjectures. We now turn to our main conjectures. Our first goal is to give a definition of a ribbon category QExc which looks like a quantum deformation of Exc . In particular we need skein relations which deform the Jacobi relation, the classical exceptional relation, and the symmetric relation between the overcrossing and undercrossing. Our specific choices of relations will be motivated below by our main theorems.

Let QExc 𝑅,𝑣,𝑤 be the ribbon category whose objects are collections of 𝑛 points on a line, and whose morphisms spaces from 𝑛 to 𝑚 consist of 𝑅 -linear combinations of planar graphs in a rectangle with 𝑛 boundary points on the bottom, 𝑚 boundary points at the top, and consisting of trivalent vertices and 4-valent braided crossings modulo planar isotopy, the usual Reidemeister 2 and 3 relations for a crossing, the 'Reidemeister 2.5' relation saying you can pull a trivalent vertex through a crossing, and the following circle, lollipop, bigon, triangle, quantum anti-symmetry, and quantum Killing symmetry relations.

texts.orig: Warning 1.13. Although (QEJac𝑤 ) looks like an analogue of the ordinary Jacobi relation with lower order terms, it is important to emphasize that it is only the correct analogue of the Jacobi relation in the exceptional setting. In particular, we do not expect any similar relation for quantizations of non-exceptional Lie algebras.

texts.orig: Proposition 1.16 (Quantum specialization) . If 𝜆 is one of the values on the left of Table 1, then there is a full and dominant functor from QExc C ,𝑣,𝑣 𝜆 to the category of representations of the quantum version of the corresponding group, for 𝑣 in some dense subset of C .
texts.text: Proposition 1.16 (Quantum specialization) . If 𝜆 is one of the values on the left of Table 1, then there is a full and dominant functor from QExc C ,𝑣,𝑣 𝜆 to the category of representations of the quantum version of the corresponding group, for 𝑣 in some dense subset of C .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As before, the category of representations of the corresponding group on the right of Table 1 is the Cauchy completion of a quotient of QExc C ,𝑣,𝑣 𝜆 . If Conjecture 1.3 holds, then the kernel of this quotient can be characterized abstractly as the tensor ideal of negligible morphisms.
texts.text: As before, the category of representations of the corresponding group on the right of Table 1 is the Cauchy completion of a quotient of QExc C ,𝑣,𝑣 𝜆 . If Conjecture 1.3 holds, then the kernel of this quotient can be characterized abstractly as the tensor ideal of negligible morphisms.

texts.orig: Weexpect that quantum specialization holds for all 𝑣 that are not roots of unity. This conjecture could also be stated more algebraically by working over the generic point QExc C ( 𝑣 ) ,𝑣,𝑣 𝜆 . Moreover, if you replace the quantum group category with the appropriate tilting modules at roots of unity, then it should hold except at some small roots of unity.

texts.orig: Being generated as a ribbon category by 𝜏 is the same as saying that C is generated as a pivotal category by 𝜏 and the crossing. In particular, a trivalent ribbon category need not be trivalent in the sense of [MPS17] where the trivalent vertex alone is assumed to generate C as a pivotal category. Also we have weakened the non-degeneracy condition from [MPS17], replacing it with the assumption that the circle and bigon are non-zero.

texts.orig: 1.4. From classical to quantum. The classical conjectures from §1.1 and the quantum conjectures from §1.3 are related, but there are no formal implications between them. From the point of view of the quantum conjectures, the classical specialization 𝑣 = 1 is excluded by the assumption that Ψ 1 is invertible. There is a partial implication the other direction: Conjecture 1.8 implies a corresponding quantum conjecture.
texts.text: 1.4. From classical to quantum. The classical conjectures from §1.1 and the quantum conjectures from §1.3 are related, but there are no formal implications between them. From the point of view of the quantum conjectures, the classical specialization 𝑣 = 1 is excluded by the assumption that Ψ 1 is invertible. There is a partial implication the other direction: Conjecture 1.8 implies a corresponding quantum conjecture.

texts.orig: 1.5. Calculations. In §5 we calculate some properties of QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 and the corresponding 2variable knot polynomial, under the assumptions of our main conjectures.
texts.text: 1.5. Calculations. In §5 we calculate some properties of QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 and the corresponding 2variable knot polynomial, under the assumptions of our main conjectures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First we give conjectural bases for the box spaces up to 𝑛 = 6, and check that these diagrams are linearly independent (assuming Conjecture 1.4). We unconditionally give a 16-dimensional representation of the 5-strand affine braid group, and an 80-dimensional representation of the 6-strand affine braid group, obtained from the braid group actions on these 5- and 6-boundary point diagrams.
texts.text: First we give conjectural bases for the box spaces up to 𝑛 = 6, and check that these diagrams are linearly independent (assuming Conjecture 1.4). We unconditionally give a 16-dimensional representation of the 5-strand affine braid group, and an 80-dimensional representation of the 6-strand affine braid group, obtained from the braid group actions on these 5- and 6-boundary point diagrams.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using this, we give a divide-and-conquer algorithm for computing the conjectural quantum exceptional invariant of all knotted trivalent graphs of Conway width at most 6. This includes all prime knots of at most 12 crossings, and nearly all prime links of at most 12 crossings, including many non-algebraic examples.
texts.text: Using this, we give a divide-and-conquer algorithm for computing the conjectural quantum exceptional invariant of all knotted trivalent graphs of Conway width at most 6. This includes all prime knots of at most 12 crossings, and nearly all prime links of at most 12 crossings, including many non-algebraic examples.

texts.orig: Note that without assuming any of our conjectures, by Proposition 1.16 these 2-variable knot polynomials must specialize at particular values of 𝑤 to give the quantum invariants of the adjoint representations of the actual Lie algebras. To our knowledge these are the first calculations of the quantum knot polynomials of non-torus knots for the adjoint representations of any of the exceptional Lie algebras, and they are much more efficient than doing the calculations directly in 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔤 ) . (For torus knots these calculations can be done using the Rosso-Jones formula [RJ93], see [MMM16].)

texts.orig: Of these, symmetries (12) and (13) do not change any of the skein relations. The first is an artifact of the parameterization, and specifically our insistence on formulas that are balanced under 𝑤 ↔ 𝑤 -1 . The second interchanges the roles of 𝜆 and 1 -𝜆 in the quantum number formulas, and is thus a quantization of the symmetry of Exc 𝑅,𝜆 interchanging 𝜆 and 1 -𝜆 . For instance it switches two eigenvalues of the twist acting on the 4-box space (Lemma 3.12).

texts.orig: 1.7. Special values. In §6, we look at what happens in certain special cases. For many of these special cases the change of variables is not well-defined, so we will often need to return to the 𝛼 -version of the main relations.
texts.text: 1.7. Special values. In §6, we look at what happens in certain special cases. For many of these special cases the change of variables is not well-defined, so we will often need to return to the 𝛼 -version of the main relations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We begin by outlining how to prove specialization theorems in the style of Proposition 1.6, using ideas from Kuperberg's paper [Kup96, Thm. 5.1], as expanded on by [EN19, Thms. 2.1 and 2.3] and [GSZ23, Thm. 5.3].
texts.text: We begin by outlining how to prove specialization theorems in the style of Proposition 1.6, using ideas from Kuperberg's paper [Kup96, Thm. 5.1], as expanded on by [EN19, Thms. 2.1 and 2.3] and [GSZ23, Thm. 5.3].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Second in §6.2, we collect and expand upon some results about Deligne's symmetric group category, the classical 𝐺 2 and 𝐹 4 families, and the relationship between these three categories and Frobenius algebras, Hurwitz algebras, and cubic Cayley-Hamilton Jordan algebras.
texts.text: Second in §6.2, we collect and expand upon some results about Deligne's symmetric group category, the classical 𝐺 2 and 𝐹 4 families, and the relationship between these three categories and Frobenius algebras, Hurwitz algebras, and cubic Cayley-Hamilton Jordan algebras.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Third, we write down explicitly which instances of the quantum exceptional relation hold in various special cases where dim C( 4 ) < 5 and so multiple relations hold.
texts.text: Third, we write down explicitly which instances of the quantum exceptional relation hold in various special cases where dim C( 4 ) < 5 and so multiple relations hold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fourth, when 𝑣 is a 10th or 12th root of unity, Corollary 1.20 does not apply. We show that when 𝑣 = ± 1 then the classical exceptional relations must hold (see [Mou22] for some related results). We show that when 𝑣 = ± 𝑖 and an additional non-degeneracy assumption holds, the relations of the classical 𝐹 4 series must hold (see [GSZ23] for some related results). Finally we show that when 𝑣 is a primitive 3rd or 6th root of unity, then the relations of the classical 𝐺 2 series must hold (see [Boo98, Wes10, Str19, Bae01] for related results). We delay a full treatment of all root of unity cases to later work.

texts.orig: We prove the classical and quantum specialization results for the exceptional family in §6.6. Then in §6.7 we prove analogous results for the 𝐺 2 and 𝐹 4 families; see §6.2 for background on the classical 𝐺 2 and 𝐹 4 families, each of which has several specializations to Lie (super)groups. Finally we conjecture a number of coincidences of quantum group categories.
texts.text: We prove the classical and quantum specialization results for the exceptional family in §6.6. Then in §6.7 we prove analogous results for the 𝐺 2 and 𝐹 4 families; see §6.2 for background on the classical 𝐺 2 and 𝐹 4 families, each of which has several specializations to Lie (super)groups. Finally we conjecture a number of coincidences of quantum group categories.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 1.23 . The category QExc 𝑅,𝑣,𝑤 , viewed in this paper as a quantized version of the conjectural exceptional family, can also be viewed as a quantization of the 𝐹 4 family. Indeed, the main difference between the classical exceptional and 𝐹 4 families is whether the generating trivalent vertex is anti-symmetric or symmetric, and thus the relation (QEJac𝑤 ) can be viewed as a quantum deformation of the defining 𝐹 4 relation from Definition 6.41. The fact that the classical consistency fails for the 𝐹 4 family [Thu05] (see §6.2.3) has no implications about quantum consistency for QExc 𝑅,𝑣,𝑤 . Such an interpretation doesn't really work for the classical 𝐺 2 family, since the 4-box space for the classical 𝐺 2 family only has dimension 4.

Such an interpretation doesn't really work for the classical 𝐺 2 family, since the 4-box space for the classical 𝐺 2 family only has dimension 4.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1.8. Other work. There has been a great deal of work on the classical (1-parameter) exceptional series and the larger (2-parameter) Vogel plane, but relatively little on the quantized versions. On one level, some previous work has calculated universal knot invariants, which can be thought of as the knot invariants coming from the putative quantized Vogel plane, hence giving specializations to the quantum knot invariants of any adjoint representation, exceptional or not. MironovMkrtchyan-Morozov [MMM16] explain the calculation of the universal knot invariants for all 2and 3-strand torus knots. Westbury [Wes15] explains the context of the quantized Vogel plane, and gives the invariants for ( 2 , 𝑛 ) torus knots (in §6.1 of that paper). Mironov-Morozov [MM16] explain how to calculate the universal invariants for all algebraic (a.k.a. arborescent) links. Note that the class we calculate includes many non-algebraic links. The first is 818; see Appendix B.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.1. Trivalent tangles and braided trivalent categories. Let 𝑅 be a ring and let Tri 𝑅 denote the ribbon category of 𝑅 -linear combinations of framed unoriented knotted trivalent graphs. That is, its objects are points on the line, and its morphisms are formal linear combinations of framed unoriented knotted trivalent graphs with composition given by vertical gluing, and tensor product given by horizontal disjoint union. Note in particular that the trivalent vertex is rotationally invariant.

Note in particular that the trivalent vertex is rotationally invariant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several of our categories have more than one tensor generator which support a product making it a trivalent ribbon category, giving distinct functors from Tri 𝑅 . In ambiguous cases we frequently add the tensor generator to disambiguate. For instance, 𝐺 2 arises with both its defining 7dimensional representation, denoted 𝑉 ( 1 , 0 ) in our weight ordering, and its adjoint 14-dimensional representation 𝑉 ( 0 , 1 ) . Thus ( 𝐺 2 , 𝑉 ( 1 , 0 ) ) and ( 𝐺 2 , 𝑉 ( 0 , 1 ) ) denote the same category Rep ( 𝐺 2 ) but indicate different functors from SymTri , and likewise the quantized versions ( 𝐺 2 , 𝑉 ( 1 , 0 ) ) 𝑞 and ( 𝐺 2 , 𝑉 ( 0 , 1 ) ) 𝑞 are the same ribbon category but with different functors from Tri . (In principle we should follow Definition 1.18 and also include the trivalent vertex 𝜏 , but in almost all cases the only ambiguity there is the scale, which is mostly fixed by specifying 𝑏 .) We also use 𝐺 as shorthand for the category Rep ( 𝐺 ) , and similarly for the quantized versions.

texts.orig: 2.2. Diagrams, Lie algebras, and inner product conventions. Consider the symmetric ribbon category of Jacobi diagrams, Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 from Definition 1.1.
texts.text: 2.2. Diagrams, Lie algebras, and inner product conventions. Consider the symmetric ribbon category of Jacobi diagrams, Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 from Definition 1.1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1. A metric Lie algebra object in a symmetric ribbon category C is an object 𝔤 with a bracket [· , ·] : 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 → 𝔤 satisfying the Jacobi identity and antisymmetry, and a symmetric pairing (· , ·) : 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 → 1 which is invariant under the adjoint action and such that there exists (a necessarily unique) copairing 𝐶 : 1 → 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 exhibiting 𝔤 as its own dual, i.e., satisfying the zig-zag axioms. We will denote the copairing on 𝔤 ∗ using angle brackets, as ⟨· , ·⟩ : 𝔤 ∗ ⊗ 𝔤 ∗ → 1.
texts.text: Definition 2.1. A metric Lie algebra object in a symmetric ribbon category C is an object 𝔤 with a bracket [· , ·] : 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 → 𝔤 satisfying the Jacobi identity and antisymmetry, and a symmetric pairing (· , ·) : 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 → 1 which is invariant under the adjoint action and such that there exists (a necessarily unique) copairing 𝐶 : 1 → 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 exhibiting 𝔤 as its own dual, i.e., satisfying the zig-zag axioms. We will denote the copairing on 𝔤 ∗ using angle brackets, as ⟨· , ·⟩ : 𝔤 ∗ ⊗ 𝔤 ∗ → 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The strand in Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 with the trivalent vertex as the bracket and the cap and cup as the pairing and copairing is a metric Lie algebra object. Moreover it has the following well-known universal property, essentially described by Bar-Natan [BN95].
texts.text: The strand in Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 with the trivalent vertex as the bracket and the cap and cup as the pairing and copairing is a metric Lie algebra object. Moreover it has the following well-known universal property, essentially described by Bar-Natan [BN95].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 2.2. Suppose C is a symmetric ribbon category over 𝑅 with End ( 1 ) = 𝑅 and 𝔤 a metric Lie algebra object with End ( 𝔤 ) = 𝑅 . Then there exist 𝐷, 𝐵 ∈ 𝑅 and a functor Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 → C sending the strand to 𝔤 and preserving the metric Lie algebra structure.
texts.text: Lemma 2.2. Suppose C is a symmetric ribbon category over 𝑅 with End ( 1 ) = 𝑅 and 𝔤 a metric Lie algebra object with End ( 𝔤 ) = 𝑅 . Then there exist 𝐷, 𝐵 ∈ 𝑅 and a functor Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 → C sending the strand to 𝔤 and preserving the metric Lie algebra structure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose 𝔤 is a simple Lie algebra over C . Then there exists a 𝔤 -invariant bilinear form which is unique up to rescaling. Each choice of such bilinear form makes 𝔤 into a metric Lie algebra and thus gives us a symmetric functor from Jac 𝑅,𝐷,𝐵 . It's easy to see that 𝐷 = dim 𝔤 . The value of 𝐵 will depend on the choice of bilinear form. In particular, rescaling the choice of bilinear form will change the interpretation of a graph by a scalar. If the graph is isotoped so that all trivalent vertices have two strands coming in from below and one from above, then the value of the diagram is homogeneous of degree the number of local maxima in the diagram minus the number of local minima.

texts.orig: More generally given a metric Lie algebra object 𝔤 and a representation object 𝑉 , we can interpret diagrams with unoriented edges labelled by 𝔤 , oriented edges labelled by 𝑉 , and trivalent vertices corresponding to both the Lie bracket (satisfying the Jacobi and antisymmetry relations) and to the action of 𝔤 on 𝑉 , satisfying the axioms of an action. (See [BN95, §2.4], though see [CL07] for sign conventions.)

texts.orig: The main reason for using all four of these conventions at various times is to use results in the literature which already made a preferred choice. A secondary reason is that when considering the Kontsevich integral it is more natural to use the Killing form or the Convenient form, while for quantum groups it's more natural to use the Short form or the Long form. We will put subscripts on the various quantities that depend on the bilinear forms, notably the bigon parameter 𝐵 ; in these contexts, ∗ is a generic subscript that can represent any of the four options.

texts.orig: Remark 2.3 . If 𝔤 is a simple Lie superalgebra (i.e., a Lie algebra object in the category of super vector spaces), then we can similarly define a Killing form using the supertrace. However, unlike in the case of simple Lie algebras, for a superalgebra it can happen that the Killing form is identically zero. If the Killing form is non-zero then it is automatically non-degenerate, and we can again make the above definitions. In this paper we will only ever consider Lie superalgebras that have non-zero Killing form. See [FSS00, §23] for a list of which simple Lie superalgebras have zero or non-zero Killing form.

In this paper we will only ever consider Lie superalgebras that have non-zero Killing form. See [FSS00, §23] for a list of which simple Lie superalgebras have zero or non-zero Killing form.

texts.orig: Recall that the Dual Coxeter number ℎ ∨ can be defined to be half of the ratio of the Killing form to the Long form [SS70, §4.8] [BZ10], while Δ (usually denoted 𝐷 , but we use 𝐷 for certain dimensions) is the lacing number , which is the ratio of the Long form to the Short form. These two facts let us easily determine the ratio between any two of these forms. For example the Convenient form is ℎ ∨ Δ / 6 = -Δ / 𝜆 times the Short form. (Here 𝜆 = -6 / ℎ ∨ is the parameter from the introduction.) See also Table 2.

texts.orig: 2.3. Quantum group conventions. In this subsection we fix conventions for quantum groups. The details here will only come up in choosing some conventions for certain diagram categories, and in the proof of quantum specialization in §6. This section is aimed at experts wishing to compare our conventions to those in other papers.
texts.text: 2.3. Quantum group conventions. In this subsection we fix conventions for quantum groups. The details here will only come up in choosing some conventions for certain diagram categories, and in the proof of quantum specialization in §6. This section is aimed at experts wishing to compare our conventions to those in other papers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If 𝔤 is a complex semisimple Lie algebra, let 𝑈𝑠 ( 𝔤 ) denote the Drinfel'd-Jimbo quantum group, and let Rep 𝑈𝑠 ( 𝔤 ) denote its ribbon category of representations. We follow the conventions from [Saw06, MPS11]. See [Saw06, p. 2] for a comprehensive summary of how his conventions line up with those in other sources. (Thus our 𝑞 is the same as Sawin's 𝑞 and Lusztig's 𝑣 .) In particular we have variables 𝑠 and 𝑞 and the relation 𝑠 𝐿 = 𝑞 where 𝐿 is the smallest integer such that 𝐿 ( 𝜇, 𝜈 ) 𝑆 ∈ Z for any two weights 𝜇 and 𝜈 . We always consider Type I finite dimensional representations. The quantum group itself and its representation theory only depend on 𝑞 , while the braiding and the ribbon category depend on the additional choice of 𝑠 . When talking about quantum groups we will typically use the bilinear form ( 𝑣, 𝑤 ) 𝑆 where short roots have squared length two. More generally, for 𝑠 not a root of unity, the braiding between irreps 𝑉𝜇 and 𝑉𝜈 depends only on 𝑠 𝐿 ( 𝜇,𝜈 ) 𝑆 and so for 𝜇 in the root lattice (as usually happens for us) the braiding depends only on 𝑞 .

texts.orig: If 𝑠 is not a root of unity, and Λ ′ is a sublattice of the weight lattice containing the root lattice, then the representations whose weights lie in Λ ′ form a ribbon subcategory. In particular, we can talk about the adjoint subcategory corresponding to the root lattice. This subcategory is exactly the subcategory generated under tensor product, direct sums, and direct summands by the adjoint representation. By the above discussion, the adjoint subcategory as a ribbon category depends only on 𝑞 and not 𝑠 . Wewill abuse notation often by referring to subcategories supported on Λ ′ as quantum versions of the group for which representations supported on Λ ′ lift to representations of the group. For example, 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 denotes the adjoint subcategory of the category of Type I finite-dimensional representations of 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔰𝔩 2 ) .

This subcategory is exactly the subcategory generated under tensor product, direct sums, and direct summands by the adjoint representation. By the above discussion, the adjoint subcategory as a ribbon category depends only on 𝑞 and not 𝑠 . Wewill abuse notation often by referring to subcategories supported on Λ ′ as quantum versions of the group for which representations supported on Λ ′ lift to representations of the group. For example, 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 denotes the adjoint subcategory of the category of Type I finite-dimensional representations of 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔰𝔩 2 ) .

We will again abuse notation by referring to this as the quantum analogue of the corresponding semidirect product group. texts.label: text
texts.orig: We use similar conventions for quantum enveloping algebras attached to simple basic Lie superalgebras with non-zero Killing form (see Remark 2.3). We follow the definitions of [Yam94, Zha02], except that we always normalize the inner product so that the short root has length two and use 𝑞 = 𝑞𝑆 . Also note that in [Yam94] their coproduct is opposite from ours. In general the quantum enveloping algebras depend not just on the Lie superalgebra but on the choice of simple roots. For a collection of conventions for simple basic Lie superalgebras see [FSS00]. Again, as in the classical case, by the quantum analogue of a Lie supergroup we will mean the appropriate equivariantization of the appropriate subcategory of representations of the quantized enveloping algebra.
texts.text: We use similar conventions for quantum enveloping algebras attached to simple basic Lie superalgebras with non-zero Killing form (see Remark 2.3). We follow the definitions of [Yam94, Zha02], except that we always normalize the inner product so that the short root has length two and use 𝑞 = 𝑞𝑆 . Also note that in [Yam94] their coproduct is opposite from ours. In general the quantum enveloping algebras depend not just on the Lie superalgebra but on the choice of simple roots. For a collection of conventions for simple basic Lie superalgebras see [FSS00]. Again, as in the classical case, by the quantum analogue of a Lie supergroup we will mean the appropriate equivariantization of the appropriate subcategory of representations of the quantized enveloping algebra.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In fact, the only quantum supergroup which we will need to understand in detail is 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑞 . In §2.5, we will explain how this relates to 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 , and so understanding the general conventions for quantum supergroups will not be important for this paper. (Warning: in [MPS17] this category was denoted 𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑞 , but this was a poor choice of notation which is not compatible with how we denote categories attached to groups.)

texts.orig: 2.4. Explicitly comparing conventions. It can sometimes be difficult to work out what conventions another paper is using, and thus to find the right change of variables between our conventions and their conventions. In this subsection we'll explain why other conventions occur and how to find the correct change of variables.
texts.text: 2.4. Explicitly comparing conventions. It can sometimes be difficult to work out what conventions another paper is using, and thus to find the right change of variables between our conventions and their conventions. In this subsection we'll explain why other conventions occur and how to find the correct change of variables.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main source of different 𝑞 conventions in the literature is that there are variables 𝑞𝑖 for each simple root 𝜇𝑖 where the formula for 𝑞𝑖 depends on the length of 𝜇𝑖 . Our choice of 𝑞 is 𝑞 = 𝑞𝑆 corresponding to the short roots, so that 𝑞𝐿 = 𝑞 Δ 𝑆 = 𝑞 Δ . It is also common in other papers to pick 𝑞𝐿 as 𝑞 . The former convention is natural if you use ⟨· , ·⟩ 𝑆 as your inner product on the root space, while the latter convention is natural if you use ⟨· , ·⟩ 𝐿 . (More generally, there are 𝑞 variables associated to any pairing; see Remark 4.1.) Similarly, if you're studying a family like 𝔰𝔬 ( 2 𝑛 + 1 ) , if you uniformly choose 𝑞 to be 𝑞𝐿 , then 𝑞𝑛 = √ 𝑞 and if you then specialize to 𝔰𝔬 ( 3 ) you end up with a choice of 𝑞 which is neither 𝑞𝑆 nor 𝑞𝐿 (which are equal because there's only one root).

texts.orig: In particular, on the summand where 𝜉 = 𝜇 + 𝜈 , the square of the negative braiding acts by 𝑞 𝜇,𝜈 . If 𝜇 = 𝜈 and 𝜉 appears with multiplicity one, we can also ask which square root of the double braiding eigenvalue is the single braiding eigenvalue, and this can usually be worked out by looking at what happens as 𝑞 goes to 1 and determining whether 𝑉𝜉 lives in the symmetric square or the alternating square of 𝑉𝜇 . One special case is that when 𝜉 = 𝜇 + 𝜈 = 2 𝜇 : in this case by looking at the formula for the 𝑅 -matrix acting on the tensor product of highest weight vectors, we can see that the single negative braiding acts by 𝑞 -⟨ 𝜇,𝜇 ⟩ in the ordinary case, and ± 𝑞 -⟨ 𝜇,𝜇 ⟩ in the super case, where the sign is the parity of 𝜇 .

texts.orig: As usual we can choose any non-zero value of 𝑏 without changing the category. Also sending 𝑞 ↦→ -𝑞 doesn't change the category. Sending 𝑞 ↦→ 𝑞 -1 doesn't change the underlying pivotal category but does change the braiding. The standard normalization of 𝑏 which arises from thinking of 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 as the full subcategory of Temperley-Lieb generated by the second Jones-Wenzl projection is 𝑏 = 𝑞 2 + 𝑞 -2 𝑞 + 𝑞 -1 . In [MPS17] we normalize so that 𝑏 = 1.

texts.orig: 2.5.2. 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) . When 𝑞 = ± 𝑖 the diagrammatic category defined in the previous subsection comes from the supergroup 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) instead of 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) , see [MPS17]. We want to explain this correspondence in more detail to clarify conventions and to explain the relationship between the quantized versions 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑡 and 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 . This is based on the descriptions in [KR89, Sal90], but modified to match our conventions. In particular, 𝔬𝔰𝔭 ( 1 | 2 ) has two positive roots, an odd root 𝛼 and an even root 2 𝛼 , and our general convention is to normalize the short root so that ⟨ 𝛼, 𝛼 ⟩ = 2, and thus ⟨ 2 𝛼, 2 𝛼 ⟩ = 8. This is an unusual convention when studying 𝔬𝔰𝔭 ( 1 | 2 ) specifically, and leads to our formulas having slightly different 𝑞 conventions from what is in the literature.

texts.orig: To be more precise about the representation category, we pass to the Lie supergroup. The supergroup 𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) has underlying group 𝑂 ( 1 ) × 𝑆𝑝 ( 2 , R ) . The non-trivial element of the center of 𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) is (in the matrix representation above) diag (-1 , -1 , -1 ) . The order-2 elements 𝜀 𝐴 = diag (-1 , 1 , -1 ) and 𝜀 𝐵 = diag ( 1 , -1 , 1 ) are not central in the supergroup, but instead parity elements : involutions so that conjugation by them gives the parity map (on, say, the Lie superalgebra). There is also a subgroup 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) (or equivalently the quotient 𝑃𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) ); that group has a unique parity element 𝜀 𝐴 .

texts.orig: When looking at the representations of a Lie supergroup, one typically studies a pair of a supergroup 𝐺 and a fixed parity element 𝜀 ∈ 𝐺 , and denotes Rep ( 𝐺,𝜀 ) the category of representations where 𝜀 acts by the parity map [Del02]. For Rep ( 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) , 𝜀𝐴 ) , this imposes a restriction on the parity of the representations: the allowed representations are 𝑉 (-1 ) 𝜇 2 𝜇 . On the other hand, Rep ( 𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) , 𝜀 𝐵 ) is isomorphic to Rep ( 𝔬𝔰𝔭 ( 1 | 2 )) , with 𝜀 𝐵 acting by parity. Rep ( 𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) , 𝜀𝐴 ) is likewise not a trivalent category.

texts.orig: Fixing conventions for the quantum version of 𝔬𝔰𝔭 ( 1 | 2 ) is somewhat miserable. In particular, since we use ( 𝑥,𝑦 ) 𝑆 for our quantum parameter conventions instead of ( 𝑥,𝑦 ) 𝐿 which agrees with the usual pairing defined by supertrace, there will be some strange factors of four. Instead of dealing with these conventions head-on, we explain the correct parameterization for 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑡 indirectly. Any deformation 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑡 will again be a trivalent category with dim Hom ( 𝑋 ⊗ 2 , 𝑋 ⊗ 2 ) = 3, and hence 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 for some change of variables between 𝑞 and 𝑡 . As discussed in §2.4, the easiest way to find this change of variables is to look at the action of HTw on the trivalent vertex. In 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 this trivalent twist factor is given by -𝑞 2 . (This follows from the formulas in the 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 section, but can also be seen by noting that the square of the braiding acts on Hom ( 𝑉 ( 2 )⊗ 𝑉 ( 2 ) , 𝑉 ( 2 ) ) by 𝑞 4 (§2.4) and the square root must be -𝑞 2 because as 𝑞 → 1 we get the antisymmetric Lie bracket on the adjoint representation.) In 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑡 , we see that the square of the braiding acts on Hom ( 𝑉 -1 ⊗ 𝑉 -1 , 𝑉 -1 ) by 𝑡 ⟨ 1 𝛼 𝐸 + , 1 𝛼 𝐸 + + 2 𝜌 ⟩ 𝑆 = 𝑡 ⟨ 1 𝛼 𝐸 + , 2 𝛼 𝐸 + ⟩ 𝑆 = 𝑡 4 , and hence the braiding acts by 𝑡 2 . (More generally, the eigenvalues for the negative crossing on Hom ( 𝑋 ⊗ 2 , 𝑋 ⊗ 2 ) for 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 are ( 𝑞 4 , -𝑞 2 , 𝑞 -2 ) while for 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) 𝑡 they are ( 𝑡 4 , 𝑡 2 , -𝑡 -2 ) .) texts.orig: The Cauchy completion of this category is exactly the usual golden category (also called the Fibonacci category) with simple objects 1 and 𝑋 with 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 𝑋 ⊕ 1. Indeed, there's clearly a full and dominant functor from the category defined by generators and relations to the algebraic category, and the functor is faithful (and thus an equivalence after Cauchy completion) because the dimensions of Hom spaces on both sides are given by the Fibonacci numbers. See [MPS17, Remark 4.13].

texts.orig: Note that as a pivotal category Rep ( 𝑆 3 ) takes a functor from 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝜁 12 (where 𝜁 12 = 𝑒 2 𝜋𝑖 / 12 ), but this functor is not a ribbon functor because they have different braidings (the former is symmetric while the latter is not) [MPS17, Ex. 8.6]. This functor is clearly essentially surjective, and is full because of the formula simplifying the crossing. However, this functor is not faithful, because Rep ( 𝑆 3 ) is semisimple, and so every negligible morphism in 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝜁 12 must be killed and we see that Rep ( 𝑆 3 ) is the semisimplification of 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝜁 12 as a pivotal category but not as a ribbon category. The smallest negligible morphism in 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝜁 12 lives in the 5-box space.

texts.orig: (The notation means, for instance, that 𝑅 = 𝑢 -2 .) Notice that 𝑅 3 acts by multiplication by 𝑢 -2 . (Indeed, 𝑅 3 does not permute the strands, and is equivalent by Reidemeister moves to changing the framing on the upper-right strand by -1.) Since 𝑅 3 acts by a nonzero scalar, we have that 𝑅 acts diagonalizably on the space spanned by these six diagrams, and in particular on the nonzero space of relations among these six diagrams in C . Thus we must have a relation which is an eigenvector for the action of 𝑅 with eigenvalue 𝜆 with 𝜆 3 = 𝑢 -2 . We can then pick a square root 𝜇 of 𝜆 -1 such that 𝜇 3 = 𝑢 , and then pick 𝑣 = ± √ 𝜇 . These are the only two values of 𝑣 satisfying 𝜆 = 𝑣 -4 and 𝑣 6 = 𝑢 .

Thus we must have a relation which is an eigenvector for the action of 𝑅 with eigenvalue 𝜆 with 𝜆 3 = 𝑢 -2 . We can then pick a square root 𝜇 of 𝜆 -1 such that 𝜇 3 = 𝑢 , and then pick 𝑣 = ± √ 𝜇 . texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3.4 . Typically, if dim C( 4 ) = 5 there will only be a one-dimensional space of relations among the six diagrams appearing in (19), and so there will be a unique relation of the form (19) up to rescaling. However, if dim C( 4 ) < 5 or if the six diagrams happen to span a strict subspace of C( 4 ) (as will happen in many of the cases considered in §6), then the vector space of relations will be more than one-dimensional. In this space of relations, relations of the form of (19) are those which are eigenvectors for the action of the operator 𝑅 . So, for example, if the space of relations among the six diagrams is 2-dimensional, and the eigenvalues for the action of 𝑅 are distinct, then C would have exactly two relations of the form of (19) up to rescaling.
texts.text: Remark 3.4 . texts.orig: Figure 2. Values excluded from the main theorems, in the ( 𝑣, 𝑤 ) plane, as in Figure 1. Note that some of the excluded values are only seen in the ( 𝑣, 𝛼 ) coordinates, since the change of variables fails. Red: denominator in (QEJac𝑤 ) or in 𝑑 vanishes, no version of QExc 𝑅,𝑣,𝑤 is defined. Brown: 𝑑 vanishes. Orange: 𝑏 vanishes, not a trivalent category. Green: 𝑣 10 = 1, exception to second part of Lemma 3.6. Purple: 𝑣 a primitive 4th or 12th root of unity, derivation of (QECross𝑤 ) fails. Cyan: action of HTw is not diagonalizable by Lemma 3.12. Black: Expected basis for 4box space is not linearly independent, as in Lemma 3.10. These black lines correspond to known existing quantum group lines, but satisfy more than one version of (QEJac𝑤 ); see §6.3. Gold crosses are the points with specializations that give the golden category, which is particularly exceptional; see Lemma 6.68. The change of variables is not invertible when 𝑣 is a root of unity of order 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 6 , 8 , 12, all called out for other reasons.

By Lemma 3.10, HTw does not satisfy a quadratic equation on this subspace, so this cubic polynomial is the minimal polynomial and its roots are all eigenvalues. The construction here is given by the Kontsevich integral 𝑍 . The Kontsevich integral was first defined for unframed knots [BN95], and later extended to framed trivalent graphs [MO97, CL07, Dan10]. We briefly recall its properties. We will specialize for now to the case corresponding to the adjoint representation of the Lie algebra, removing the usual distinction between different kinds of edges.

texts.orig: Remark 4.2 . Given Conjecture 1.8, to deduce a version of Conjecture 1.15 with a non-infinitesimal deformation (valued in, say, complex numbers rather than a power series ring), we would have to get control over the coefficients in the power series that arise in the functor Qeval constructed above. This has been done for the Kontsevich integral 𝑍 [Le99, APŠ11]. The evaluation algorithm implicit in eval would also have to have computable bounds that are not too large. Most reasonable evaluation algorithms do give such bounds, but we do not have an evaluation algorithm.
texts.text: Remark 4.2 . Most reasonable evaluation algorithms do give such bounds, but we do not have an evaluation algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We (NS and DPT) have been able to reproduce some of the calculations, but for several of the larger calculations we haven't been able to reproduce the full details (either because they require a larger computer or because there were some additional tricks used in the calculation that weren't well documented). In particular, we were unable to reproduce the calculation of det 𝑀 6 in §5.2, the calculation of the operators 𝑇 in §5.3, and the calculations of the null-spaces in §5.6. Although the level of rigor in this section is lower than in the rest of the paper, we do not have serious concerns about the validity of these calculations, which pass a number of sanity tests.

At this point we can only give heuristics for choosing these sets of diagrams; in the next section we will establish that they are linearly independent, but it is beyond the scope of this paper to show that they span. (This is probably at least as hard as showing that the quantum exceptional relation suffices to evaluate all closed diagrams.) texts.orig: Note that the Lie algebra 𝔣 4 works better for computing dimensions of invariant spaces than, say, 𝔢 7 or 𝔢 8, which have representations that degenerate in 𝔤 ⊗ 3 and 𝔤 ⊗ 4 , respectively, while 𝔢 6 has Dynkin diagram automorphisms which results in additional complications. The number for 𝑛 = 9 is also presumably correct for the exceptional series, since it can be computed as dimHom 𝔣 4 ( 𝔣 ⊗ 4 4 → 𝔣 ⊗ 5 4 ) , i.e., only representations appearing in 𝔣 ⊗ 4 4 are relevant.

texts.orig: In order to write our proposed bases compactly, we introduce the following notations. Let 𝐷 ∗ 𝑘 denote the set of all 𝑘 inequivalent rotations of the diagram 𝐷 , including 𝐷 itself (so when 𝐷 has 𝑛 boundary points, 𝑘 divides 𝑛 ). Let 𝐷 ∗ 𝑘 ♣ denote the set of the first 𝑘 counterclockwise rotations of the diagram 𝐷 (including 𝐷 itself), even though these do not exhaust all of the inequivalent rotations. In particular, 𝐷 ∗ 1 ♣ denotes just the diagram itself.

texts.orig: Thefollowing heuristic produces these proposed bases. Consider the classical setting where the overcrossing and undercrossing are equal. Now focus on the case of a diagram whose underlying graph is a tree with 𝑘 leaves. If you fix the last vertex as a root vertex and label the remaining vertices clockwise as 𝑥 1 , 𝑥 2 , . . . 𝑥 𝑘 -1, then such a diagram can be interpreted as a bracket formula like [ 𝑥 2 , [ 𝑥 1 , 𝑥 3 ]] in the free Lie algebra on 𝑘 -1 letters. But due to the Jacobi and antisymmetry relation we should expect these diagrams to span a space that's smaller than naively given by counting binary trees. In fact, by the Witt Dimension Formula applied to words of degree ( 1 , 1 , . . . ) [Wit37, Satz 3], they span an ( 𝑘 -2 ) ! dimensional space which has as a basis the diagrams obtained by taking the left-associated bracket formula [[[[ 𝑥 1 , 𝑥 2 ] , 𝑥 3 ] , . . . ] , 𝑥 𝑘 -1 ] and permuting the 𝑘 -2 legs { 𝑥 2 , . . . , 𝑥 𝑘 -1 } arbitrarily.

texts.orig: More generally, for forests pick a partition of the boundary points into parts with at least two elements. For each such partition, and for each part with 𝑘 elements, pick an abstract (unembedded) tree with those 𝑘 points as the leaves, and form the ( 𝑘 -2 ) ! trees (still unembedded) obtained by the symmetric group action on the leaves. For each such unembedded tree, pick an embedding in the plane which involves as few crossings as possible. Finally in the quantum setting we choose over- and under-crossings arbitrarily.

texts.orig: Proof. The relation (QESq𝑤 ) lets us turn a crossing into a square (with a factor) plus some simpler diagrams. Furthermore, a square next to a pentagon can be turned into a single pentagon plus simpler diagrams, by Lemma 5.4 below. We thus get a triangular change-of-basis matrix, with coefficients rational functions of 𝑣 and 𝑤 , from B braided 𝑘 to B planar 𝑘 or vice versa. □

texts.orig: Remark 5.5 . Mousaaid [Mou22, Eq. 4.3] explicitly identified a relation among 5-boxes for classical E8 which rewrites the pentagon in terms of the braided basis. Above we used Eq. (24) to write a twisted tree in terms of a pentagon plus lower-order terms, but we could also use that equation the other way to extract a 5-box relation for the quantum exceptional family which will presumably specialize to Mousaaid's relation.

texts.orig: Remark 5.7 . We check that these matrices satisfy a number of sanity checks in the 'Verifying Matrices' part of the Mathematica notebook QES-Knot-Polynomials.nb . In particular, rotating 𝑛 times is the identity, the cupcap squared is 𝑑 times the cupcap, applying a cup and then fusing is zero (by the lollipop relation), the braiding composed with the inverse braiding is the identity, and applying a fork or cup and then an appropriate crossing does have the correct twist values.

texts.orig: 5.4. Representations of braid groups. The affine 𝑛 -strand braid group has generators 𝜌 and 𝛽 , with relations 𝜌 𝑛 = 1, 𝛽𝜌 ± 𝛽𝜌 ∓ 𝛽 = 𝜌 ± 𝛽𝜌 ∓ 𝛽𝜌 ± 𝛽𝜌 ∓ , and 𝛽𝜌 𝑖 𝛽𝜌 -𝑖 = 𝜌 𝑖 𝛽𝜌 -𝑖 𝛽 for all 𝑖 = 2 , . . . , 𝑛 -2. At this point, we may verify that the matrices 𝜌 = 𝜌𝑛 and 𝛽 = 𝛽𝑛 provide a representation of the affine 𝑛 -strand braid group merely by multiplying out the matrices.

Typically, we may choose to think about a diagrammatically defined tangle invariant as a morphism of planar algebras Tangle →Q for some planar algebra Q , sending the crossing to a suitable element of Q 4. With our limited set of operations in the target planar algebra, we can only compute the (conjectural) invariants of links which we can generate from the crossing using available operations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 5.9. The width of a generic embedding of a planar graph in the plane is the maximum number of intersections with a horizontal line. The width of an abstract planar graph is the minimum width over all generic embeddings.
texts.text: Definition 5.9. The width of a generic embedding of a planar graph in the plane is the maximum number of intersections with a horizontal line. The width of an abstract planar graph is the minimum width over all generic embeddings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The basic polyhedron of a 4-valent planar graph is the graph obtained by repeatedly collapsing all digons to a point. The Conway width of a 4-valent planar graph is the width of its basic polyhedron. The Conway width of a link diagram is the Conway width of the graph where all crossings are replaced by 4-valent vertices, and the Conway width of a link is the minimum over all diagrams. In particular, algebraic (a.k.a. arborescent) links are those with Conway width 2, and there are no links with Conway width 4.
texts.text: The basic polyhedron of a 4-valent planar graph is the graph obtained by repeatedly collapsing all digons to a point. The Conway width of a 4-valent planar graph is the width of its basic polyhedron. The Conway width of a link diagram is the Conway width of the graph where all crossings are replaced by 4-valent vertices, and the Conway width of a link is the minimum over all diagrams. In particular, algebraic (a.k.a. arborescent) links are those with Conway width 2, and there are no links with Conway width 4.

texts.orig: Proof. One can enumerate the basic polyhedra with 𝑛 vertices using Brinkmann and McKay's plantri program [BM07, BGG + 05], with the command plantri -qda -c2 ⟨ 𝑛 + 2 ⟩ . All basic polyhedra with less than 12 vertices have width at most 6, and of the 12 vertex basic polyhedra all but 12C and 12G (using the naming scheme from [Cau82]) have width at most 6. These graphs, made into links, are the exceptions described above. □

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is an obvious greedy algorithm to compute link invariants using our limited operations (implicitly showing a diagram has Conway width 6): repeatedly collapse digons; when that is not possible apply 𝑚 4 , 4 , 1 to merge two 4-boxes into a 6-box, and afterwards hope that 𝑚 6 , 4 , 2 suffices to combine all remaining 4-boxes into that 6-box. This works on all prime knots up to 12 crossings. (For a handful of knots, if you apply 𝑚 4 , 4 , 1 'in the wrong place' it is possible to get stuck-relabeling the strands and trying again quickly succeeds.) Our tabulations are based on this algorithm.

texts.orig: It would be desirable to verify directly that these conjectural computed invariants specialize correctly to the quantum link invariants of the adjoint representations of the exceptional Lie algebras. (We will prove unconditionally in §6.6 that these do in fact specialize correctly-but it would be nice to be able to check computations.) Unfortunately, these invariants have previously been rather hard to compute, so there is little available to check against. The Lie algebra 𝔰𝔩 2 lies on the exceptional curve, and so the 2nd colored Jones polynomial (that is, colored by the 3dimensional adjoint representation) should be the specialization to 𝑣 = 𝑞 1 / 3 and either 𝑤 = ± 𝑞 4 / 3 or 𝑤 = ± 𝑞 -1 (both work). This is indeed the case for all prime links up to 9 crossings, and all prime knots up to 10 crossings.

texts.orig: 5.7. Heuristic calculations using the Kontsevich integral. In this section we briefly sketch how one can more quickly guess several of the results from §5.6 assuming Deligne's conjecture, using the Kontsevich integral construction and Cohen-de-Man's computations. In addition to what we used in §4, we will use the version of the Kontsevich integral that incorporates another line type labelled by a representation. In particular, we need that if the quadratic Casimir of a Lie algebra object 𝔤 acts on an irrep 𝑉 by a scalar 𝐶 then the framing change operator Fr acts on 𝑉 by the power series 𝑒 ℎ𝐶 / 2 , as in Remark 4.1. The calculations in this section generalize those in the last section of [TW01].
texts.text: 5.7. Heuristic calculations using the Kontsevich integral. In this section we briefly sketch how one can more quickly guess several of the results from §5.6 assuming Deligne's conjecture, using the Kontsevich integral construction and Cohen-de-Man's computations. In addition to what we used in §4, we will use the version of the Kontsevich integral that incorporates another line type labelled by a representation. In particular, we need that if the quadratic Casimir of a Lie algebra object 𝔤 acts on an irrep 𝑉 by a scalar 𝐶 then the framing change operator Fr acts on 𝑉 by the power series 𝑒 ℎ𝐶 / 2 , as in Remark 4.1. The calculations in this section generalize those in the last section of [TW01].

texts.orig: In a quantum group, we expect 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑛 1 to have commuting representations of 𝑈𝑞 ( 𝔤 ) and of 𝐵𝑛 . Of course representations of 𝐵𝑛 are much more complicated than representations of 𝑆𝑛 , so the simple plethysm-type formulas aren't going to make a lot of sense. The four different copies of (for instance) 𝑋 2 inside of 𝑋 ⊗ 3 1 , which appear in three different representations of 𝑆 3 in the plethysm formulas, presumably come from a single irreducible representation of 𝐵 3 which happens to become reducible at 𝑞 = 1.

texts.orig: To sort out which representation is which, we look at the center of 𝐵𝑛 , for which we take as generator the negative full-twist operator Tw 𝑛 (see (16)). In general, braid group representations break up as a direct sum of representations with different eigenvalues of this full twist. It is also related to the quadratic Casimir. To get that relation more precise, it's a little better to look at a related operator, the framing change operator Fr, which does a positive Reidemeister 1 move to the entire bundle of strands. This is the same as doing a positive full twist and adding a positive kink (Reidemeister 1) to each strand individually. As such, we have

texts.label: section_header
texts.text: 6. Special values

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal of this section is to analyze several special cases. First, we introduce the classical 𝐹 4 and 𝐺 2 families and Deligne's symmetric group family. Second, we record which instances of the quantum exceptional relation hold in particular examples when more than one relation holds. Third, when 𝑣 is a 10th or 12th root of unity, the proof of our main theorem breaks down. We delay a full treatment of what happens when 𝑣 is a 10th or 12th root of unity to later work; here we will restrict ourselves to some results characterizing the classical 𝐺 2, 𝐹 4, and exceptional families, and a classification under the additional assumption that the crossing is symmetric. Fourth, if 𝔤 is a Lie algebra lying in the exceptional, 𝐹 4, or 𝐺 2 families we show that an appropriate quantum group category takes a full and dominant functor from the QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 . Assuming a weak version of quantum sufficiency, this implies that these quantum group categories are the Cauchy completion (i.e., additive and Karoubi completion) of the quotient of QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 for that 𝑣 and 𝑤 by negligible morphisms. Finally we conjecture a number of equivalences between quantum group categories which correspond to intersections between lines corresponding to different quantum groups. These may be thought of as an exceptional analogue of Level-Rank duality in the spirit of Beliakova and Blanchet [Bla00, BB01].
texts.text: The goal of this section is to analyze several special cases. First, we introduce the classical 𝐹 4 and 𝐺 2 families and Deligne's symmetric group family. Second, we record which instances of the quantum exceptional relation hold in particular examples when more than one relation holds. Third, when 𝑣 is a 10th or 12th root of unity, the proof of our main theorem breaks down. We delay a full treatment of what happens when 𝑣 is a 10th or 12th root of unity to later work; here we will restrict ourselves to some results characterizing the classical 𝐺 2, 𝐹 4, and exceptional families, and a classification under the additional assumption that the crossing is symmetric. Fourth, if 𝔤 is a Lie algebra lying in the exceptional, 𝐹 4, or 𝐺 2 families we show that an appropriate quantum group category takes a full and dominant functor from the QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 . Assuming a weak version of quantum sufficiency, this implies that these quantum group categories are the Cauchy completion (i.e., additive and Karoubi completion) of the quotient of QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 for that 𝑣 and 𝑤 by negligible morphisms. Finally we conjecture a number of equivalences between quantum group categories which correspond to intersections between lines corresponding to different quantum groups. These may be thought of as an exceptional analogue of Level-Rank duality in the spirit of Beliakova and Blanchet [Bla00, BB01].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section is somewhat longer than expected. In part this is simply because we need to discuss what is happening in each of four families (the Exceptional family, the 𝐺 2 family, the 𝐹 4 family, and the symmetric group family, corresponding to 𝑣 being plus or minus a primitive first, third, fourth, or twelfth root of unity). The other reason is that the 𝐹 4 family section is particularly long. A lot of what we've written follows what was done by Gandhi, Savage, and Zainoulline [GSZ23] for the group 𝐹 4 itself, but not for the rest of the 𝐹 4 family, so we need to combine their approach with results of Benkart, Elduque, and Cvitanović.

A lot of what we've written follows what was done by Gandhi, Savage, and Zainoulline [GSZ23] for the group 𝐹 4 itself, but not for the rest of the 𝐹 4 family, so we need to combine their approach with results of Benkart, Elduque, and Cvitanović.

texts.orig: 6.1. Specialization theorems. We begin by outlining how to prove specialization theorems in the style of Proposition 1.6; this is used in Propositions 6.40, 6.63, and 6.96. These classical specialization theorems can then be used to prove quantum specialization theorems in the style of Proposition 1.16, as in Propositions 6.97, 6.100, and 6.103.

texts.orig: Definition 6.1. Suppose that C is a category and that D is a semisimple category. We say that F : C → D is dominant if the functor from the Cauchy completion of C to D guaranteed by the universal property of Cauchy completion is essentially surjective.
texts.text: Definition 6.1. Suppose that C is a category and that D is a semisimple category. We say that F : C → D is dominant if the functor from the Cauchy completion of C to D guaranteed by the universal property of Cauchy completion is essentially surjective.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that if F : C → D is full then it is dominant if and only if every simple object of D is isomorphic to a direct summand of an object in the image of F .

texts.orig: Remark 6.4 . Once you see that the adjoint of the trivalent vertex is plus or minus its reflection it is easy to work out which sign arises, because the composition of the adjoint of the trivalent vertex with the vertex must be a positive multiple of the identity: the trivalent vertex is adjoint to its reflection if the bigon value is positive and adjoint to the negative of its reflection otherwise. In particular, there's a sign error in [Kup96, Thm. 5.1]: since at 𝑞 = 1 the value of the bigon is negative, the adjoint of the trivalent vertex should be negative its reflection. (Also compare to [Jon16, §5].) (For 𝐺 2, we have that 𝜎 = -1, so Kuperberg is choosing the pairing to be positive definite with 𝛿 = 1, so 𝜎𝛿 = -1.) This sign issue is completely inessential to the proof because all that's actually used is that the subcategory generated by the trivalent vertex is closed under taking adjoints, which will be true for either sign.
texts.text: Remark 6.4 . Once you see that the adjoint of the trivalent vertex is plus or minus its reflection it is easy to work out which sign arises, because the composition of the adjoint of the trivalent vertex with the vertex must be a positive multiple of the identity: the trivalent vertex is adjoint to its reflection if the bigon value is positive and adjoint to the negative of its reflection otherwise. In particular, there's a sign error in [Kup96, Thm. 5.1]: since at 𝑞 = 1 the value of the bigon is negative, the adjoint of the trivalent vertex should be negative its reflection. (Also compare to [Jon16, §5].) (For 𝐺 2, we have that 𝜎 = -1, so Kuperberg is choosing the pairing to be positive definite with 𝛿 = 1, so 𝜎𝛿 = -1.) This sign issue is completely inessential to the proof because all that's actually used is that the subcategory generated by the trivalent vertex is closed under taking adjoints, which will be true for either sign.

texts.orig: Proposition 6.5. Suppose that 𝐴 is a commutative or anti-commutative real algebra with an associative definite symmetric bilinear form. Then the functor G 𝐴 : Tri C → Rep C ( 𝐺𝐴 ) is full and dominant.
texts.text: Proposition 6.5. Suppose that 𝐴 is a commutative or anti-commutative real algebra with an associative definite symmetric bilinear form. Then the functor G 𝐴 : Tri C → Rep C ( 𝐺𝐴 ) is full and dominant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. The Cauchy completion of the image of G 𝐴 is a 𝐶 ∗ -subcategory C of Rep C ( 𝐺𝐴 ) and in particular is semisimple and hence abelian. By post-composing with the fiber functor of Rep ( 𝐺𝐴 ) , we see that C has a fiber functor to vector spaces. By Tannaka-Krein duality, we see that C is the category of representations of a group 𝐻 with 𝐺𝐴 ⊆ 𝐻 ⊆ O ( 𝐴 ) , where 𝑂 ( 𝐴 ) is the orthogonal group of the vector space 𝐴 with its symmetric billinear form. But the multiplication is a map of 𝐻 -representations, hence 𝐻 ⊆ Aut ( 𝐴 ) = 𝐺𝐴 . Thus 𝐻 = 𝐺𝐴 and the functor is full.
texts.text: Proof. The Cauchy completion of the image of G 𝐴 is a 𝐶 ∗ -subcategory C of Rep C ( 𝐺𝐴 ) and in particular is semisimple and hence abelian. By Tannaka-Krein duality, we see that C is the category of representations of a group 𝐻 with 𝐺𝐴 ⊆ 𝐻 ⊆ O ( 𝐴 ) , where 𝑂 ( 𝐴 ) is the orthogonal group of the vector space 𝐴 with its symmetric billinear form. But the multiplication is a map of 𝐻 -representations, hence 𝐻 ⊆ Aut ( 𝐴 ) = 𝐺𝐴 . Thus 𝐻 = 𝐺𝐴 and the functor is full.

texts.orig: We now turn our attention to quantum specialization results. Suppose that 𝐺 is a compact group and 𝐺𝑞 is corresponding quantum group category deforming Rep ( 𝐺 ) . We fix a functor F : Tri C → Rep ( 𝐺 ) and let 𝑋 denote the image of the strand under F . We will say that a deformation F 𝑞 : Tri C → 𝐺𝑞 defined for 𝑞 in some open dense subset of C is given by rational functions if the image of any trivalent tangle written as a matrix with respect to the standard bases of the representations (i.e., the PBW basis of lowering operators applied to the highest weight vector) has matrix entries given by rational functions of 𝑞 . 5

texts.orig: Remark 6.7 . A more principled approach here is to use an algebro-geometric viewpoint instead of a complex-analytic one, and introduce a rational form of the quantum group over Q [ 𝑞, 𝑞 -1 ] and to pass between different rings in the following arguments. See [EN19, GSZ23] where this is approach is done carefully. We take a complex-analytic viewpoint so that we don't need to worry about some roots which could appear in the formulas for 𝑣 and 𝑤 in terms of 𝑞 . In our examples, 𝑣 and 𝑤 will turn out to be rational functions of 𝑞 , but this depends on explicit calculations.

texts.orig: Proposition 6.8. Let 𝐺 be a compact group with a self-dual simple object 𝑋 ∈ Rep ( 𝐺 ) that appears with multiplicity one inside 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 . Then the induced functor F : Tri C → Rep ( 𝐺 ) has a deformation F 𝑞 : Tri C → 𝐺𝑞 given by rational functions. Moreover, we may assume WLOG that after rescaling the self-duality the value of the bigon can be taken to be any rational function of 𝑞 that we like.
texts.text: Proposition 6.8. Let 𝐺 be a compact group with a self-dual simple object 𝑋 ∈ Rep ( 𝐺 ) that appears with multiplicity one inside 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 . Then the induced functor F : Tri C → Rep ( 𝐺 ) has a deformation F 𝑞 : Tri C → 𝐺𝑞 given by rational functions. Moreover, we may assume WLOG that after rescaling the self-duality the value of the bigon can be taken to be any rational function of 𝑞 that we like.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. We fix a family of objects 𝑋𝑞 in each 𝐺𝑞 deforming 𝑋 . As in [EN19, Lemma 2.4], there exists a family of morphisms deforming the self-duality and the trivalent vertex given by rational functions (indeed by Laurent polynomials). Since 𝑋 is irreducible and since 𝑋 appears with multiplicity one inside 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 , these morphisms are unique up to rescaling.
texts.text: Proof. We fix a family of objects 𝑋𝑞 in each 𝐺𝑞 deforming 𝑋 . texts.orig: In Rep ( 𝐺 ) , the object 𝑋 is symmetrically self-dual (since 𝐺 is compact) and the trivalent vertex has rotational eigenvalue 1 (since 𝐺 is a group). This remains true under deformation, since rotational eigenvalues form a discrete set (and thus cannot change under deformation).
texts.text: In Rep ( 𝐺 ) , the object 𝑋 is symmetrically self-dual (since 𝐺 is compact) and the trivalent vertex has rotational eigenvalue 1 (since 𝐺 is a group). This remains true under deformation, since rotational eigenvalues form a discrete set (and thus cannot change under deformation).

texts.orig: Finally the braiding is given by rational functions by the 𝑅 -matrix formula. Since the trivalent vertex, the self-duality, and the braiding generate all morphisms in Tri C , we have a family of functors F 𝑞 : Tri C → 𝐺𝑞 given by rational functions.
texts.text: Finally the braiding is given by rational functions by the 𝑅 -matrix formula. Since the trivalent vertex, the self-duality, and the braiding generate all morphisms in Tri C , we have a family of functors F 𝑞 : Tri C → 𝐺𝑞 given by rational functions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the functor constructed in the first paragraph is given by rational functions, it sends the bigon to some rational function in 𝑞 times the identity morphism. If we rescale the choice of self-duality by any rational function then the functor will still be given by rational functions, and so we can choose the self-duality in order to assure that the value of the bigon is our favorite rational function. □

texts.orig: Proof. By Proposition 6.8 we have a family of functors F 𝑞 : Tri C → 𝐺𝑞 given by rational functions. By Corollary 1.20, after rescaling the self-duality to match our conventions for the bigon, outside of a finite set of points this functor factors through QExc C ,𝑣 ( 𝑞 ) ,𝑤 ( 𝑞 ) for some 𝑣 ( 𝑞 ) and 𝑤 ( 𝑞 ) . Moreover, by looking at the proof of Corollary 1.20, it's easy to verify that 𝑣 ( 𝑞 ) and 𝑤 ( 𝑞 ) are given by algebraic functions. Thus by choosing appropriate branch cuts in C each is given by an analytic function of 𝑞 . □

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. For fixed 𝑛 the map is surjective on the 𝑛 -box space at 𝑞 = 1 by assumption, and is thus surjective for 𝑞 in a Zariski open dense set, and thus a dense set in the ordinary complex topology. Therefore the functor is full on a countable intersection of dense sets, and thus by the Baire Category Theorem the functor is full on a dense set.

texts.orig: 6.2. Other 1 -parameter classical families. In this section we introduce three families of symmetric ribbon categories, namely Sym ( 𝑑 + 1 ) , 𝐺 2 ( 𝑑 ) , and 𝐹 4 ( 𝑑 ) . It will turn out these are related to QExc 𝑅,𝑣,𝑤 with 𝑣 being a primitive 12th root, a primitive 3rd root, or a primitive 4th root of unity, respectively, much as the classical exceptional family is related to 𝑣 = 1.
texts.text: 6.2. Other 1 -parameter classical families. In this section we introduce three families of symmetric ribbon categories, namely Sym ( 𝑑 + 1 ) , 𝐺 2 ( 𝑑 ) , and 𝐹 4 ( 𝑑 ) . It will turn out these are related to QExc 𝑅,𝑣,𝑤 with 𝑣 being a primitive 12th root, a primitive 3rd root, or a primitive 4th root of unity, respectively, much as the classical exceptional family is related to 𝑣 = 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In all three cases a parallel phenomenon occurs: if 𝑋 is the strand, then in the additive completion 1 ⊕ 𝑋 admits the structure of an algebra object satisfying certain axioms, and that moreover these three families are universal for symmetric ribbon categories with such an algebra. We will see that for the Sym family 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a commutative Frobenius algebra object, for the 𝐺 2 family 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a Hurwitz algebra object, and for the 𝐹 4 family 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a unital Jordan algebra object with a trace that moreover satisfies the cubic Cayley-Hamilton identity.
texts.text: In all three cases a parallel phenomenon occurs: if 𝑋 is the strand, then in the additive completion 1 ⊕ 𝑋 admits the structure of an algebra object satisfying certain axioms, and that moreover these three families are universal for symmetric ribbon categories with such an algebra. We will see that for the Sym family 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a commutative Frobenius algebra object, for the 𝐺 2 family 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a Hurwitz algebra object, and for the 𝐹 4 family 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a unital Jordan algebra object with a trace that moreover satisfies the cubic Cayley-Hamilton identity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 6.2.1. Sym ( 𝑡 ) . In this section we recall a description of Deligne's symmetric group family 𝑆 spec 𝑡 [Del07] and a closely related trivalent symmetric ribbon category Sym ( 𝑡 ) , and the relationship between these categories and commutative Frobenius algebras. This is based on NS's diagrammatics for 𝑆𝑡 which are described in [KS20] and inspired by the diagrammatic description given by Harriger [Har21]. Since this topic is somewhat tangential to the main point of the paper, and are not difficult given the results already in [Har21], we will quickly state the correct theorems without proof. More details should appear eventually in an expanded version of [Har21]joint with NS. We will distinguish two slightly different variants, the special version 𝑆 spec 𝑡 and the normalized version 𝑆 norm 𝑡 .
texts.text: 6.2.1. Sym ( 𝑡 ) . In this section we recall a description of Deligne's symmetric group family 𝑆 spec 𝑡 [Del07] and a closely related trivalent symmetric ribbon category Sym ( 𝑡 ) , and the relationship between these categories and commutative Frobenius algebras. This is based on NS's diagrammatics for 𝑆𝑡 which are described in [KS20] and inspired by the diagrammatic description given by Harriger [Har21]. Since this topic is somewhat tangential to the main point of the paper, and are not difficult given the results already in [Har21], we will quickly state the correct theorems without proof. More details should appear eventually in an expanded version of [Har21]joint with NS. We will distinguish two slightly different variants, the special version 𝑆 spec 𝑡 and the normalized version 𝑆 norm 𝑡 .
