texts.orig: Remark 6.12 . When 𝑡 is not a positive integer, the Cauchy completion of 𝑆 spec 𝑡 is a semisimple abelian category. When 𝑡 is a positive integer, then the quotient of the Cauchy completion of 𝑆 spec 𝑡 by negligibles agrees with the representation category Rep ( 𝑆𝑡 ) . (See §2.5.5 for the case 𝑡 = 3.) When 𝑡 is a positive integer there is also an 'abelian envelope' of 𝑆 spec 𝑡 which is a nonsemisimple abelian category [Del07, CO11, Har16]. The construction of this abelian category is much more subtle than the diagrammatic version, and we will have no need to consider it.

texts.orig: Definition 6.14. A commutative Frobenius algebra over 𝑘 is a unital commutative associative algebra 𝐴 over 𝑘 with a trace tr: 𝐴 → 𝑘 such that tr ( 𝑥𝑦 ) is a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form. A commutative Frobenius algebra object in a symmetric ribbon category is a unital commutative associative algebra object 𝐴 with a trace tr: 𝐴 → 1 such that tr ◦ 𝜇 is the evaluation of a symmetric self-duality ev on 𝐴 .
texts.text: Definition 6.14. A commutative Frobenius algebra over 𝑘 is a unital commutative associative algebra 𝐴 over 𝑘 with a trace tr: 𝐴 → 𝑘 such that tr ( 𝑥𝑦 ) is a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form. A commutative Frobenius algebra object in a symmetric ribbon category is a unital commutative associative algebra object 𝐴 with a trace tr: 𝐴 → 1 such that tr ◦ 𝜇 is the evaluation of a symmetric self-duality ev on 𝐴 .

texts.orig: If 𝐴 is a Frobenius algebra object and tr ( 1 ) ≠ 0 then 𝑎 ↦→ tr ( 𝑎 ) · 1 / tr ( 1 ) : 𝐴 → 𝐴 is a projection, and so 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 for some sub-object 𝑋 . The restriction of multiplication composed with projection onto 𝑋 gives a map 𝑀 : 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 . It is easy to see that the Karoubi envelope of 𝑆 norm 𝑡 with the object 𝑋 and the map 𝑀 is a trivalent category, and we can easily read off its defining generators and relations by expanding 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 in the defining relations of 𝑆 norm 𝑡 .

texts.orig: Remark 6.20 . If 𝐴 is an algebra object in a symmetric monoidal category C with a multiplication 𝜇 and some additional structures such as a unit 𝑢 : 1 → 𝐴 , or trace tr: 𝐴 → 1 , we will still write expressions 'in elements' using dummy variables (as above). This notation will cause no confusion provided that each variable appears exactly once in each term. We can sum together such terms or set such sums equal to each other. For example, we can write 𝑥 ↦→ tr ( 1 𝑥 ) to mean the composition tr ◦ 𝜇 ◦ ( 𝑢 ⊗ id ) : 𝐴 = 𝑘 ⊗ 𝐴 → 𝑘 . Or we can write the Jacobi identity for a Lie algebra object (using bracket as the algebra operation) as [ 𝑥, [ 𝑦, 𝑧 ]] = [[ 𝑥,𝑦 ] , 𝑧 ] + [ 𝑦, [ 𝑥, 𝑧 ]] . Similarly, if 𝐴 is endowed with a symmetric self-duality, we will write a duality pairing 𝐴 ⊗ 𝐴 → 𝑘 using the notation ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦 ⟩ .

texts.orig: Remark 6.27 . There are three reasonable normalizations for 𝑏 in the definition of Sym ( 𝑑 + 1 ) : the 'spherical' choice 𝑏 = 𝑑 -1 √ 𝑑 + 1 , the 'special' choice 𝑏 = 𝑑 -1 𝑑 + 1 , and the 'normalized' choice 𝑏 = 𝑑 -1. When 𝑑 + 1 ≠ 0 the resulting categories are equivalent by changing the self-duality, but when 𝑑 + 1 = 0 more care should be taken.

texts.orig: In particular, when 𝑑 = -1, our Sym ( 0 ) does not agree with Deligne's 𝑆 0. This is because Deligne's 𝑆 spec 0 has a special Frobenius algebra object with tr ( 1 ) = 0, while our Sym ( 0 ) is equivalent to 𝑆 norm 0 and thus has a normalized Frobenius algebra object with tr ( 1 ) = 1 but 𝜇 ◦ Δ = 0. In particular, in our category the permutation object breaks up as a direct sum of the standard and the trivial, whereas for Deligne's the permutation will be a nontrivial extension of the trivial and the standard. A similar story applies for 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) 𝑞 at 𝑞 = ± 𝑖 but for (not necessarily commutative) Frobenius algebra objects in a (not necessarily symmetric) tensor category. We hope to address this further in a later paper.

A vector product algebra is a (non-unital) 𝑘 -algebra 𝐴 with a symmetric bilinear form with a symmetric monoidal functor 𝐺 2 ( 𝑑 ) → Vec 𝑘 sending the object to 𝐴 and the trivalent vertex to the multiplication. We will also call it a 𝐺 2 -algebra . Similarly we will refer to an object 𝐴 in a symmetric ribbon category C together with a functor 𝐺 2 ( 𝑑 ) → C as a 𝐺 2 -algebra object in C .

A vector product algebra is a (non-unital) 𝑘 -algebra 𝐴 with a symmetric bilinear form with a symmetric monoidal functor 𝐺 2 ( 𝑑 ) → Vec 𝑘 sending the object to 𝐴 and the trivalent vertex to the multiplication. texts.orig: Example 6.30 . R 3 with the standard bilinear form and multiplication given by the cross product is a vector product algebra (or 𝐺 2-algebra) over R . For this example 𝑑 = 3. If we endow R 3 with the defining action of 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) then the standard bilinear form and the cross product are 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) -equivariant. Thus the defining representation of 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) is a 𝐺 2-algebra object in Rep ( 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 )) .

texts.orig: Example 6.32 . The real numbers, complex numbers, quaternions, and octonions, with their standard norms, are Hurwitz algebras over R . Moreover, in these examples the bilinear form is positive definite, from which it follows that they're all division algebras with the inverse of 𝑥 given by 𝑥 / 𝑁 ( 𝑥 ) . Their complexifications ( C , C ⊕ C , 𝑀 2 ( C ) , and the bi-octonions) are Hurwitz algebras over C . (Since the quadratic form is no longer definite, they are not division algebras.)

texts.orig: If 𝐴 is a Hurwitz algebra object, then we have a trace map which is dual to the unit map. Moreover, the first axiom of a Hurwitz algebra object tells you that tr (·) 1 : 𝐴 → 𝐴 is an idempotent, and so in the idempotent completion of C , we have 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 for some sub-object 𝑋 . The restriction of multiplication composed with projection onto 𝑋 gives a map 𝑀 : 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 .
texts.text: If 𝐴 is a Hurwitz algebra object, then we have a trace map which is dual to the unit map. Moreover, the first axiom of a Hurwitz algebra object tells you that tr (·) 1 : 𝐴 → 𝐴 is an idempotent, and so in the idempotent completion of C , we have 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 for some sub-object 𝑋 . The restriction of multiplication composed with projection onto 𝑋 gives a map 𝑀 : 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 .

texts.orig: Proposition 6.36 ([Str19, Thm. 25]) . Suppose C is an idempotent complete symmetric ribbon category with a Hurwitz algebra object 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 and dim 𝑋 = 𝑑 . Then C takes a symmetric monoidal functor from 𝐺 2 ( 𝑑 ) sending the strand to 𝑋 and the trivalent vertex to the restricted multiplication 𝑀 (i.e., 𝑋 is a 𝐺 2 -algebra object in C ).

texts.orig: Remark 6.37 . Explicitly, the multiplication on the Hurwitz algebra 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is given by ( 𝑎, 𝑥 )( 𝑏,𝑦 ) = ( 𝑎𝑏 - ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦 ⟩ , 𝑎𝑦 + 𝑏𝑥 + 𝑥𝑦 ) . In particular, the component of the multiplication map in 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 1 is not the pairing 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 1 induced by the symmetric self-duality of 𝐴 , but instead those two maps are negatives of each other. One good way to think about this is that ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦 ⟩ = tr ( 𝑥𝑦 ) and so they're negatives of each other when restricted to 𝑋 , since 𝑥 = -𝑥 in 𝑋 . In general, there are some parameters involved in turning a non-unital algebra 𝑋 into a unital algebra 1 ⊕ 𝑋 ; see the proof of Proposition 6.51.

texts.orig: If 𝐴 is one of the four real Hurwitz algebras from Example 6.32, then let 𝐺𝐴 denote the group of unital algebra automorphisms of the real Hurwitz algebra 𝐴 which preserve the bilinear form, as in §6.1. (It's not difficult to see that these groups can also be described as the groups of automorphisms of the corresponding real vector product algebra 𝑋 which preserve the bilinear form.) Since the inner product on the real Hurwitz algebras are positive definite, it follows that 𝐺𝐴 is a compact group, and that its category of complex representations has a unitary structure. The groups 𝐺𝐴 are trivial, 𝑆 2, 𝑆𝑂 ( 3 ) , and the compact form of 𝐺 2, respectively, as shown in Table 9 on page 87.

texts.orig: A tracial Jordan algebra is a Jordan algebra together with a unit 𝑘 → 𝐴 satisfying the usual unit axioms, and a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form ⟨· , ·⟩ : 𝐴 ⊗ 𝐴 → 𝑘 which is associative, ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦𝑧 ⟩ = ⟨ 𝑥𝑦, 𝑧 ⟩ , and satisfies ⟨ 1 , 1 ⟩ = 1. In a tracial Jordan algebra we let tr ( 𝑥 ) ≔ ⟨ 𝑥, 1 ⟩ . Associativity guarantees that ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦 ⟩ = tr ( 𝑥𝑦 ) and tr (( 𝑥𝑦 ) 𝑧 ) = tr ( 𝑥 ( 𝑦𝑧 )) .
texts.text: A tracial Jordan algebra is a Jordan algebra together with a unit 𝑘 → 𝐴 satisfying the usual unit axioms, and a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form ⟨· , ·⟩ : 𝐴 ⊗ 𝐴 → 𝑘 which is associative, ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦𝑧 ⟩ = ⟨ 𝑥𝑦, 𝑧 ⟩ , and satisfies ⟨ 1 , 1 ⟩ = 1. In a tracial Jordan algebra we let tr ( 𝑥 ) ≔ ⟨ 𝑥, 1 ⟩ . Associativity guarantees that ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦 ⟩ = tr ( 𝑥𝑦 ) and tr (( 𝑥𝑦 ) 𝑧 ) = tr ( 𝑥 ( 𝑦𝑧 )) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since ⟨ 𝑥,𝑦 ⟩ = tr ( 𝑥𝑦 ) we will often specify a tracial Jordan algebra by specifying the trace instead of the bilinear form; of course one then needs to check that the resulting bilinear form is non-degenerate. The assumption that ⟨ 1 , 1 ⟩ = 1 = tr ( 1 ) is not essential, we could instead assume only that ⟨ 1 , 1 ⟩ ≠ 0. But this normalization is convenient and one can always rescale the bilinear form to ensure it. However there are a few places where our normalization assumption appears unexpectedly, see Remark 6.50.

texts.orig: Remark 6.54 . The coefficients in the cubic Cayley-Hamilton relation depend on our choice of normalization. That is, if 𝑀 is a 3-by-3 matrix with the normalized trace, then the sum of the eigenvalues of 𝑀 is 3 tr ( 𝑀 ) . The coefficients would be nicer if we had normalized so that tr ( 1 ) = 3 so that it agreed with the matrix trace for 3-by-3 matrices.

texts.orig: Example 6.55 . If 𝑅 is a Hurwitz algebra, 𝑀 3 ( 𝑅 ) with its standard tracial Jordan algebra structure is cubic Cayley-Hamilton. If 𝑅 is a Hurwitz algebra, let 𝐻 3 ( 𝑅 ) denote the Hermitian 3-by-3 matrices over 𝑅 . (Here Hermitian means equal to its conjugate transpose, where conjugate is the conjugate in the sense of Hurwitz algebras.) This is a Jordan subalgebra of 𝑀 3 ( 𝑅 ) and thus also cubic CayleyHamilton. The significance of 𝐻 3 ( 𝑅 ) is that, if the inner product on 𝑅 is positive-definite (as for 𝑅 = R , C , H , O ), then the inner product on 𝐻 3 ( 𝑅 ) is the Frobenius inner product and thus also positive-definite.

texts.orig: Lemma 6.58. If 𝐴 is a cubic Cayley-Hamilton tracial Jordan algebra object then its traceless part 𝑋 is cubic Cayley-Hamilton. Conversely, if 𝑋 is a cubic Cayley-Hamilton traceless Jordan algebra object, then the unique Jordan algebra structure on 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is cubic Cayley-Hamilton.
texts.text: Lemma 6.58. If 𝐴 is a cubic Cayley-Hamilton tracial Jordan algebra object then its traceless part 𝑋 is cubic Cayley-Hamilton. Conversely, if 𝑋 is a cubic Cayley-Hamilton traceless Jordan algebra object, then the unique Jordan algebra structure on 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is cubic Cayley-Hamilton.

texts.orig: In the backwards direction the same calculation run backwards shows that the tracial cubic Cayley-Hamilton axiom holds on the 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 component. We need to also check the other components. This is a tedious but straightforward check: for example, if one of the inputs is 1 and the others are 𝑋 then only the first two terms of the Cayley-Hamilton identity contribute. For the first term, each permutation contributes a trivalent vertex, while for the second term all but two permutations vanish and the remaining two contribute a trivalent vertex. Thus we get 6 -3 · 2 = 0 copies of the trivalent vertex. The other calculations are similar. □

texts.orig: If 𝐴 is real cubic Cayley-Hamilton tracial Jordan algebra with positive definite bilinear form, then let 𝐺𝐴 denote the group of unital algebra automorphisms of 𝐴 which preserve the bilinear form. (It's not difficult to see that these groups can also be described as the groups of automorphisms of the corresponding traceless Jordan algebra.) Since it preserves a positive definite form, 𝐺𝐴 is compact and the category Rep ( 𝐺𝐴 ) has a 𝐶 ∗ -structure. One can similarly define a supergroup 𝐺𝐴 of automorphisms of a tracial Jordan superalgebra, though we do not specify the notion of compactness there.

texts.orig: Proof. For 𝑑 = 0 , 2, the result follows immediately by inspection of the algebra. For 𝑑 = -2, by inspection we see that the automorphisms of 𝐽 0 | 2 are 𝑆𝐿 ( 2 ) ; this representation 𝑉 ( 1 ) should be viewed as odd, and the tensor category this generates is Rep ( 𝑆𝐿 ( 2 ) , 𝜀 = -1 ) . For 𝑑 = -1, by [BE03, Lemma 2.4] we see that the Lie superalgebra of derivations of 𝐷 2 is 𝔬𝔰𝔭 ( 1 | 2 ) , with traceless part the representation 𝑉 -1 . From the analysis in §2.5.2, it follows that the Lie supergroup is 𝑆𝑂𝑆𝑝 ( 1 | 2 ) .

texts.text: Proposition 6.63. If 𝑑 = 0 , 2 , 5 , 8 , 14 , 26 , then 𝐹 4 ( 𝑑 ) has a full and dominant functor to the category Rep ( 𝐺𝐴 𝑑 ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. For 𝑑 ≠ 2, the lollipop is zero since the traceless part of 𝐴 is a non-trivial simple 𝐺𝐴 -module. A direct calculation for 𝑑 = 2 shows the lollipop is zero in that case too. Since 𝐴 is a cubic Cayley-Hamilton tracial Jordan algebra of dimension 𝑑 + 1 with lollipop zero, we have a functor F : 𝐹 4 ( 𝑑 ) → Rep ( 𝐺𝐴 𝑑 ) . This functor is full and dominant by Proposition 6.5 and Remark 6.6. □

texts.orig: Remark 6.64 . The series of Lie algebras on the last four lines of Table 8 is well-known, particularly the construction of 𝐹 4 as 𝐻 3 ( O ) . There are descriptions of the other automorphism groups going back to Kalisch [Kal47, Thm. 6] (for 𝐻 3 ( R ) and 𝐻 3 ( H ) ) and Jacobson [Jac59] (in very large generality), but the fact that Aut ( 𝐻 3 ( C )) is not connected seems to be relatively little-noticed in the literature. The series appears prominently in the work of Freudenthal [Fre64] and Tits [Tit66] as part of their work on the magic square , and has seen many other developments and explanations

texts.orig: since then. However, almost all references only consider the square as Lie algebras and not the specific Lie groups necessary to get the correct representation theory. This is perhaps because the natural version of the magic square as Lie groups is not symmetric, as seen in the work of Deligne and Gross [DG02], who give an extended magic triangle, agreeing with the groups in Table 7 on the 𝐸 8 row, Table 8 on the 𝐹 4 row, and Table 9 on the 𝐺 2 row. (The 𝐸 6 and 𝐸 7 rows in Deligne-Gross do not give trivalent categories in our sense.)

Most of our work on this paper came before the recent developments [GSZ23, Mou22, SW22], but the results and ideas here in §6.2.3 are strongly influenced by reading [GSZ23].

texts.orig: Remark 6.65 . In [GSZ23] they find an additional pentagon relation in the kernel of the map 𝐹 4 ( 26 ) → Rep ( 𝐹 4 ) , using the fact that dim B 5 = 15 for ( 𝐹 4 , 𝑉 ( 0 , 0 , 0 , 1 ) ) . For the 𝔠 3, 𝔞 2, and 𝔞 1 lines in the 𝐹 4 family, the computations in §5.2 show that the 5-box space is at least 16-dimensional, and so there is no reason to believe this pentagon relation holds; as a result, we do not use it in our definition of 𝐹 4 ( 𝑑 ) .

texts.orig: Proof. If the first four diagrams are linearly dependent then we're in one of the first three cases by the previous lemma. Otherwise, we have relations simplifying both the crossing and the square in terms of the first four diagrams. This means that C is generated by the trivalent vertex and dim C( 4 ) ≤ 4, and thus by a modification of [MPS17, §5] we see that C is a quotient of ( 𝐺 2 ) 𝑞 as a pivotal category. By [Kup94, §2.3] (see also [MPS17, §6]), after possibly replacing 𝑞 with 𝑞 -1 (which doesn't change the pivotal category) we may assume the braiding on ( 𝐺 2 ) 𝑞 is the standard one.

texts.orig: Specifically, in [MPS17] we only considered non-degenerate trivalent categories, but this can be avoided as follows. The argument that the pentagon reduces still applies, so all small faces reduce, and non-elliptic diagrams span all C( 𝑘 ) . If the 10 non-elliptic diagrams in C( 5 ) are independent then Kuperberg [Kup94] says that C is a quotient of ( 𝐺 2 ) 𝑞 . If there's a relation between these ten diagrams, the relation must be negligible. By [MPS17] either there's a single relation and the category is a quotient of ( 𝐺 2 ) 𝜁 10 , or the non-degenerate quotient of C is an 𝐴𝐵𝐴 category which contradicts C being braided. □

texts.orig: Proof. ByProposition 3.3 and Lemma 3.5, we satisfy (QEJac𝛼 ) for some 𝑣, 𝛼 . Since C is symmetric, looking at the trivalent twist we see 𝑣 6 = ± 1, and hence 𝑣 is a 12th root of unity. If 𝑣 = ± 1 then 𝑋 is a Lie algebra object satisfying the classical Exceptional relations by Corollary 6.83. If 𝑣 is a third or sixth root of unity then 𝑋 is a 𝐺 2-algebra object by Proposition 6.85. If 𝑣 is a primitive 4th root of unity and 𝑏 + [ 3 ] 𝛼 ≠ 0 then 𝑋 is an 𝐹 4-algebra object by Proposition 6.87. If 𝑣 is a primitive 4th root of unity and 𝑏 + [ 3 ] 𝛼 = 0 then by Lemma 6.89, 𝑋 is again an 𝐹 4-algebra object (using for the first time that C is symmetric in addition to 𝑣 12 = 1).

texts.orig: Corollary 6.94. Suppose that 𝐺 is a compact Lie group of non-zero dimension, and 𝑋 is a non-trivial symmetrically self-dual irrep 𝐺 of dimension 𝑑 with dimHom ( 𝑋 ⊗ 3 , 1 ) = 1 and dimHom ( 𝑋 ⊗ 4 , 1 ) ≤ 5 . Then one of the following holds:
texts.text: Corollary 6.94. Suppose that 𝐺 is a compact Lie group of non-zero dimension, and 𝑋 is a non-trivial symmetrically self-dual irrep 𝐺 of dimension 𝑑 with dimHom ( 𝑋 ⊗ 3 , 1 ) = 1 and dimHom ( 𝑋 ⊗ 4 , 1 ) ≤ 5 . Then one of the following holds:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The map 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is anti-symmetric, and 𝑋 𝔤 is a Lie algebra with a compatible 𝐺 -action.
texts.text: The map 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is anti-symmetric, and 𝑋 𝔤 is a Lie algebra with a compatible 𝐺 -action.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The map 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is anti-symmetric, and 𝑋 is not the adjoint representation, in which case there's a Hurwitz algebra 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 with a compatible 𝐺 -action.
texts.text: The map 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is anti-symmetric, and 𝑋 is not the adjoint representation, in which case there's a Hurwitz algebra 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 with a compatible 𝐺 -action.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The map 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is symmetric, in which case there's a cubic Cayley-Hamilton Jordan algebra 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 with a compatible 𝐺 -action.
texts.text: The map 𝑋 ⊗ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is symmetric, in which case there's a cubic Cayley-Hamilton Jordan algebra 𝐴 1 ⊕ 𝑋 with a compatible 𝐺 -action.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. This follows from Proposition 6.91, except that in the case where the multiplication is commutative, we need to rule out the possibility that 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a commutative Frobenius algebra object. In this case we would have a functor Sym ( 𝑡 ) → Rep ( 𝐺 ) , and since the latter is unitary, this functor factors through the quotient by negligible morphisms. Hence we get a functor Rep ( 𝑆𝑛 ) → Rep ( 𝐺 ) for some 𝑛 , from which it follows that 𝐺 is a subgroup of 𝑆𝑛 which contradicts the assumption on dim 𝐺 . □
texts.text: Proof. This follows from Proposition 6.91, except that in the case where the multiplication is commutative, we need to rule out the possibility that 1 ⊕ 𝑋 is a commutative Frobenius algebra object. In this case we would have a functor Sym ( 𝑡 ) → Rep ( 𝐺 ) , and since the latter is unitary, this functor factors through the quotient by negligible morphisms. Hence we get a functor Rep ( 𝑆𝑛 ) → Rep ( 𝐺 ) for some 𝑛 , from which it follows that 𝐺 is a subgroup of 𝑆𝑛 which contradicts the assumption on dim 𝐺 . □

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An interesting corollary here is that there must be some cubic Cayley-Hamilton algebra attached to each Lie group in the 𝐹 4 family. We already explicitly identified these algebras, so this doesn't provide any new results, but it explains why we got lucky and were able to find such Jordan algebras.
texts.text: An interesting corollary here is that there must be some cubic Cayley-Hamilton algebra attached to each Lie group in the 𝐹 4 family. We already explicitly identified these algebras, so this doesn't provide any new results, but it explains why we got lucky and were able to find such Jordan algebras.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 6.6. Specializations to exceptional Lie algebras. Suppose that 𝑉 is a self-dual representation of a quantum group ribbon category such that there's a unique map 𝑉 ⊗ 𝑉 → 𝑉 and such that 𝑉 ⊗ 𝑉 has no more than 5 distinct summands all of which are multiplicity free. Then Theorems 1 and 2 apply, and some instance of the quantum exceptional relations must hold. In this section we explicitly identify a number of quantum group categories as quotients of the quantum exceptional series following this outline.
texts.text: 6.6. Specializations to exceptional Lie algebras. Suppose that 𝑉 is a self-dual representation of a quantum group ribbon category such that there's a unique map 𝑉 ⊗ 𝑉 → 𝑉 and such that 𝑉 ⊗ 𝑉 has no more than 5 distinct summands all of which are multiplicity free. Then Theorems 1 and 2 apply, and some instance of the quantum exceptional relations must hold. In this section we explicitly identify a number of quantum group categories as quotients of the quantum exceptional series following this outline.

texts.orig: Definition 6.95. We say that a simple Lie (super)algebra 𝔤 is an Exceptional Lie algebra if it appears in Table 7, an expanded version of Table 1 (excluding the trivial group). We let 𝜆 = -6 / ℎ ∨ where ℎ ∨ is the dual Coxeter number. For the Lie algebras in the table (excluding 𝔬𝔰𝔭 ( 1 | 2 ) ), let 𝔨 denote the compact form of 𝔤 and consider the group 𝐺 𝔨 = Aut ( 𝔨 ) of Lie algebra automorphisms. Explicitly, this is the semidirect product of the adjoint form of the compact Lie group 𝐾 attached to 𝔨 by the group of Dynkin diagram automorphisms. Since the group of Dynkin diagrams automorphisms is discrete, the Lie algebra of 𝐺 𝔨 is 𝔨 . See Table 7 for more details about the values of 𝜆 , the groups, and the representations. As in §2.2, Δ is the ratio of the squared length of the long roots to the squared length of the short roots.

As in §2.2, Δ is the ratio of the squared length of the long roots to the squared length of the short roots.

texts.orig: Recall that the Killing form (and hence any of the other normalizations we use for the inner product) restricted to 𝔨 is negative definite, and that any automorphism of 𝔨 automatically preserves the Killing form since it is defined by a matrix trace of compositions of brackets. Hence the construction of 𝐺 𝔨 from 𝔨 follows exactly the procedure from §6.1.

Thus, by Proposition 6.82, the classical exceptional relation with parameter 𝜆 holds for some 𝜆 . By Lemma 3.14 we see that 2 𝜆 and 2 ( 1 -𝜆 ) are the unique non-zero eigenvalues of the ladder operator on 𝔤 ⊗ 𝔤 not coming from the summands 𝔤 or 1. But, by Corollary 2.9, the eigenvalue on the summand 𝑉 2 𝜃 is -12 / ℎ ∨ . Thus (by the symmetry exchanging 𝜆 and 1 -𝜆 ) we may take 𝜆 = -6 / ℎ ∨ . Hence the functor descends to one out of Exc C ,𝜆 .

This result is in the style of the first fundamental theorem of invariant theory; one would also like an explicit characterization of the kernel of this quotient, which we do not have. texts.orig: We would like to apply Lemma 3.12. First we need to verify that ( 𝑣, 𝛼 ) as 𝑣 varies does not lie entirely on an excluded line from Lemma 3.12. This can be seen by, for instance, taking the limit of 𝑑 as 𝑣 → 1: for the excluded lines we get 3 or -2, which is not dim 𝔤 . Therefore, at all but finitely many values of 𝑞 we can apply Lemma 3.12, to see that, up to symmetry, we may take 𝑤 = 𝑞 -Δ . To see that we can take the correct root of 𝑣 6 and get 𝑣 = 𝑞 -Δ / 𝜆 , look near 𝑞 = 1 and use Lemma 3.14 and continuity; see also §4. We thus get a functor from QExc C ,𝑣,𝑤 to Rep ( 𝐺 𝔨 ) 𝑞 .

texts.orig: Remark 6.98 . In particular, we see that if 𝔤 is Exceptional and not 𝔞 1, the braiding on the 4box space of Rep ( 𝐺 𝔨 ) 𝑞 has an eigenvector for the braiding of eigenvalue -1. This eigenvector corresponds to projection onto the simple summand of 2 𝔤 complimentary to 𝔤 , named 𝑋 2 by Deligne. (In the case of 𝔞 1, that summand vanishes.) From the Kontsevich integral point of view, this follows from Lemma 2.10.

texts.orig: 6.7. Specializations to the quantum 𝐹 4 and 𝐺 2 families. Similar results hold for the 𝐹 4 and 𝐺 2 series using similar arguments. For 𝐹 4 itself this appears in [SW22, GSZ23], but in general we have to be a bit careful about choosing the correct group 𝐺 attached to the Lie algebra 𝔤 following §6.2. For 𝐺 2 this is already in [Kup94, Kup96].

texts.orig: Remark 6.101 . Note that 𝐴 2 with its adjoint representation is both an Exceptional Lie algebra and an 𝐹 4 Lie algebra. However, the action of PSU ( 3 ) ⋊ Z / 2 Z on 𝔰𝔩 ( 3 ) is subtly different because they come from automorphisms of different algebra structures. Namely the non-trivial element of Z / 2 Z acts by negative transpose in the exceptional case and by transpose in the 𝐹 4 case.

If the quantum sufficiency conjecture holds, we could deduce the braided equivalences shown in Table 10. Each triple ( 𝐹, Γ , 𝑞 ) indicates an equivariantization of the subcategory of the (semisimplified) quantum group representation category for the Lie algebra Γ in the family 𝐹 at the root of unity 𝑞 tensor generated by an appropriate irreducible (as listed earlier in this section). The order of the root of unity determines the level, but this does not completely specify the (braided) category, which depends on the actual value of 𝑞 -although of course all such categories are Galois conjugates. We only list one equivalence out of each Galois conjugacy class. We use again the shorthand 𝜁 𝑛 = exp ( 2 𝜋𝑖 / 𝑛 ) to write 𝑞 . Each conjectured equivalence would take the tensor generator to the tensor generator.

Specifically we exclude values where 𝑣 is a primitive root of unity of order 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 8 , 10 , 12, 𝑤 = ± 1, 𝑤 = ± 𝑣 𝑘 for 𝑘 = -5 , 6, or 𝑤 = ± 𝑖𝑣 𝑘 for 𝑘 = -2 , 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Warning 6.104. In this discussion, we are taking our conjectural diagrammatic categories and semisimplifying them at the listed root of unity. In order to translate these conjectural equivalences into statements about the semisimplified category of tilting modules for the Lusztig quantum group we would need to know that the semisimplified diagram category and the semisimplified tilting module category were equivalent. Even in cases where such an equivalence is known at roots of unity, it will still fail for a few small roots of unity. So one should be especially skeptical about the conjectures below in the semisimplified tilting module setting if the root of unity is small.
texts.text: Warning 6.104. In this discussion, we are taking our conjectural diagrammatic categories and semisimplifying them at the listed root of unity. In order to translate these conjectural equivalences into statements about the semisimplified category of tilting modules for the Lusztig quantum group we would need to know that the semisimplified diagram category and the semisimplified tilting module category were equivalent. Even in cases where such an equivalence is known at roots of unity, it will still fail for a few small roots of unity. So one should be especially skeptical about the conjectures below in the semisimplified tilting module setting if the root of unity is small.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 6.105 . The case of 𝐹 4 at level 4, ( 𝐸 8 , 𝐹 4 , 𝜁 9 52 ) ( 𝐹 4 , 𝐹 4 , 𝜁 7 52 ) (circled in Figure 1) is especially interesting. This is a categorical version of the automorphism of the fusion ring of 𝐹 4 level 4 from [Gan02, §3.8]. Composing this equivalence with its own Galois conjugate gives a braided equivalence (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 52 ) (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 25 52 ) (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 -1 52 ) (with some permutation of the representations) and hence a braided reversing auto-equivalence of (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 52 ) . This braided reversing auto-equivalence may be of interest to the study of exotic subfactors. NS and KM noticed that 𝐹 4 at level 4 has dimensions which are related to the dimensions of objects in the Extended Haagerup subfactor [BMPS12], and Edie-Michell [EM22, Question 7.2] strengthened this observation to ask whether the center of the Extended Haagerup fusion categories is an Evans-Gannon grafting [EG11] of 𝐹 4 level 4 using a braiding-reversing autoequivalence. Evans-Gannon grafting has not been rigorously defined at the level of ribbon categories, and moreover we need to assume the quantum sufficiency conjecture in order to construct the autoequivalence Edie-Michell requires. We will explain this in more detail in future work.
texts.text: Remark 6.105 . The case of 𝐹 4 at level 4, ( 𝐸 8 , 𝐹 4 , 𝜁 9 52 ) ( 𝐹 4 , 𝐹 4 , 𝜁 7 52 ) (circled in Figure 1) is especially interesting. This is a categorical version of the automorphism of the fusion ring of 𝐹 4 level 4 from [Gan02, §3.8]. Composing this equivalence with its own Galois conjugate gives a braided equivalence (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 52 ) (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 25 52 ) (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 -1 52 ) (with some permutation of the representations) and hence a braided reversing auto-equivalence of (( 𝐹 4 ) 4 , 𝜁 52 ) . This braided reversing auto-equivalence may be of interest to the study of exotic subfactors. NS and KM noticed that 𝐹 4 at level 4 has dimensions which are related to the dimensions of objects in the Extended Haagerup subfactor [BMPS12], and Edie-Michell [EM22, Question 7.2] strengthened this observation to ask whether the center of the Extended Haagerup fusion categories is an Evans-Gannon grafting [EG11] of 𝐹 4 level 4 using a braiding-reversing autoequivalence. Evans-Gannon grafting has not been rigorously defined at the level of ribbon categories, and moreover we need to assume the quantum sufficiency conjecture in order to construct the autoequivalence Edie-Michell requires. We will explain this in more detail in future work.
